docs.title: Scope And Evidence Boundary
docs.text:
This synthesis uses existing exploration reports and memos only. No new data analysis, preprocessing stages, parquet inspection, or LLM calls were run.

Gate 3 is the primary strategic context. T19, T20/T21, T22/T23, and V2 failed or are missing; their topics are treated as explicit gaps, not inferred evidence.

docs.title: Emerging Paper Narrative
docs.text:
Between 2024 and 2026, SWE and software-adjacent technical postings did not show a robust collapse of junior demand. The stronger evidence is AI-era skill-surface expansion: postings broadened around AI/tool/platform/workflow requirements across returning companies, metros, and adjacent technical occupations, while seniority labels and YOE floors diverged as measurement signals.

This should be framed as employer-side posting-content restructuring, not employment effects or a clean causal estimate of AI on jobs.

docs.title: Top Findings
docs.text: Rank Finding Evidence strength Why it matters 1 Within-company AI/tool expansion Strong T16: common companies moved broad AI 3.74% -> 23.23% and AI-tool 2.23% -> 19.80%, with 91-92% of AI/tool change decomposed as within-company. This is the lead mechanism result. 2 SWE-amplified, adjacent-parallel expansion Strong T18: SWE and adjacent technical roles move far more than controls. This reframes the paper away from SWE-only restructuring. 3 Geographic diffusion Strong T17: all 26 eligible metros increased broad-AI and AI-tool prevalence. The shift is not only Bay Area/Seattle/NYC. 4 Requirement and tech breadth expansion across seniority Strong to moderate T11/T14/V1: breadth rises across J1-J4 and S1/S4. Caveat: many text measures are LLM-cleaned subset claims. 5 Seniority label-vs-YOE divergence Moderate, high conceptual value T08/T16/T30/T15: explicit entry labels and low-YOE floors identify different rows and sometimes move in opposite directions. 6 Domain/technology dominates seniority in posting structure Strong within LLM subset T09/T15/V1: archetype/domain explains much more representation structure than seniority. 7 Senior roles broaden, but management-decline story is unsupported Moderate T11: senior breadth and mentorship/coordination rise; direct-report management remains rare. T21 missing prevents a lead senior-archetype claim. 8 Signal is not only boilerplate, but requirement force is unresolved Moderate for not-only-boilerplate, missing for force T13/V1/T16 reduce simple boilerplate/new-company explanations. T22 missing means screened versus aspirational requirements remain unknown.

docs.title: Recommended RQ Evolution
docs.text:
RQ1: How much did AI/tool/platform and requirement-breadth language expand in SWE postings from 2024 to 2026, and does that expansion survive company, source, geography, and occupation controls?

RQ2: Is the expansion uniquely SWE-specific, or is it shared across software-adjacent technical occupations relative to controls?

RQ3: How do seniority labels, YOE floors, and requirement profiles diverge, and what does that imply for measuring junior and senior labor-market restructuring?

RQ4: Which parts of AI/tool/platform language are screened requirements, preferred skills, aspirational language, employer branding, or templates?

RQ5: Do employer-side AI/tool requirements outpace worker-side AI usage benchmarks? This should remain a hypothesis until T23-equivalent evidence exists.

docs.title: What Changed From The Original Framing
docs.text:
The original RQ1 emphasis on junior-share decline is weakened or contradicted. Aggregate and geography/cross-occupation panels often show junior definitions rising; common-company panels split J1/J2 labels downward and J3/J4 low-YOE upward. The stronger junior result is boundary ambiguity plus scope expansion.

The original RQ2 downward-migration frame is too narrow. Requirement breadth rises for junior and senior definitions, and domain/technology structure dominates seniority. The better framing is broad skill-surface expansion, with targeted migration as a secondary analysis.

The original senior-role redefinition claim needs demotion. Wave 2 supports senior complexity, mentorship, coordination, and technical breadth; it does not support a clean decline in management language. T21 failed, so senior archetype claims should be cautious.

The original employer-worker divergence RQ remains important but untested. Posting-side AI/tool evidence is strong, but T23 failed and requirement force is unknown.

docs.title: Method Recommendations
docs.text:
What worked:

  • T30 seniority panel discipline: J1/J3 and S1/S4 distinctions prevented false junior/senior conclusions.
  • Within-2024 calibration: essential for separating source noise from cross-period movement.
  • Common-company decomposition: T16 turned AI/tool expansion into a credible mechanism result.
  • Cross-occupation control design: T18 clarified that the paper is about technical-work expansion, not SWE uniqueness.
  • LLM-cleaned text with raw sensitivity: T13/T12 avoided raw boilerplate traps.
  • V1 verification: independent checks caught construct drift and citation risks.
  • Company caps and aggregator exclusions: necessary for text, topic, and entry-share claims.

What did not work or should be avoided:

  • Single junior-share estimates.
  • Asaniczka native associate as a junior proxy.
  • Current normalized-title S3 as a senior-title prevalence measure.
  • Raw title/new-title counts as clean temporal evidence.
  • Raw-description topic modeling without boilerplate controls.
  • Generic junior-senior semantic convergence as a lead claim.
  • Remote-work analysis until the all-zero 2026 remote field is audited.
  • Broad multi-task agents after OOM/stalls; use smaller single-task recovery scripts.

docs.text: Lead claim Support Caveats Missing checks Returning companies sharply expanded AI/tool posting language. T16 common-company AI/tool decomposition; V1 verified Wave 2 AI prevalence. Company canonicalization; broad versus narrow AI; binary mentions not force. V2 verification; T22 requirement-force validation. Expansion is SWE-amplified but adjacent-parallel. T18 SWE/adjacent/control DiD. Occupation classification and adjacent heterogeneity. V2 verification; boundary-specific follow-up for data/ML/data engineering. AI/tool diffusion is geographically broad. T17 all 26 eligible metros positive. Metro-known frame; source and domain composition; multi-location exclusions. LLM-cleaned subset for requirement breadth; length confounds; regex measures. Expanded LLM coverage or careful subset language; T22 force validation. Junior collapse is not supported as a lead fact. T30, T08, T17, T18; T16 split by construct. J1/J2 source-sensitive; J3/J4 are YOE constructs; unknown seniority large. T19 temporal stability; T20 boundary modeling. Seniority labels and YOE floors diverge. T08 low-YOE mostly unknown/mid-senior; T16 J1 down/J3 up; T15 nearest-neighbor split. Source and company composition; title code under-labeling. Plausible threat from V1/T11 caveats; not direct evidence. T13/V1/T16 argue against all-boilerplate, but force is unknown. T22 or interview evidence required. Employer-worker divergence is plausible. Posting-side numerator is strong. No external usage comparison. T23-equivalent benchmark analysis required.

docs.title: Recommended Analysis-Phase Priorities
docs.text:
1. Estimate company fixed-effect models for broad AI, AI-tool, tech breadth, and requirement breadth.
  2. Estimate cross-occupation DiD models for SWE, SWE-adjacent, and controls, with alternative adjacent group definitions.
  3. Build a compact metro diffusion figure and model with metro fixed effects or domain-mix controls.
  4. Formalize requirement-breadth models with length controls, section filtering, company caps, aggregator exclusion, and LLM-coverage disclosure.
  5. Treat seniority as a measurement chapter: J1 versus J3/J4, S1 versus S4, unknown-seniority profiling, and label-vs-YOE divergence.
  6. Recover a small T22-style requirement-force analysis before claiming hiring-bar changes.
  7. Run a lightweight T19 stability check before making rate or flow claims about the 2026 scraped window.
  8. Attempt T23 only after tool-specific posting measures and external usage benchmarks are cleanly aligned.
  9. Expand or explicitly bracket scraped LLM-cleaned coverage before final semantic/archetype claims.

docs.title: Explicit Gaps From Failed Tasks
docs.text:
• T19 missing: no temporal-rate stability, scraped-window consistency, posting-age, backlog, or flow evidence. Avoid annualized rate claims.
  • T20 missing: no seniority-boundary classifier, associate-distance model, or formal low-YOE unknown profiling.
  • T21 missing: no senior-role deep dive. Do not lead with senior archetype redefinition beyond T11-supported complexity/mentorship evidence.
  • T22 missing: no ghost/aspirational/template or screened-requirement force analysis.
  • T23 missing: no employer-requirement / worker-usage divergence evidence.
  • T28/T29 not run: no full domain-stratified decomposition and no LLM-authorship artifact test.
  • V2 missing: Wave 3 T16/T17/T18 are synthesized by Gate 3 but lack independent verification comparable to V1.

docs.title: Suggested Figures And Tables
docs.text:
1. Within-company decomposition of broad AI, AI-tool, tech breadth, requirement breadth, J1, and J3.
  2. Cross-occupation DiD slope plot or table for SWE, adjacent technical roles, and controls.
  3. Metro heatmap or ranked slope plot showing all-metro AI/tool increases.
  4. Requirement and technology breadth by T30 seniority definitions, with LLM coverage note.
  5. J1/J2 versus J3/J4 seniority-boundary panel, including unknown-seniority composition.
  6. Domain/archetype versus seniority structure figure from T09/T15.
  7. AI/Python/platform technology network change from T14.
  8. Claims-and-caveats table separating posting-language claims from hiring-bar claims.
  9. Interview mechanism table mapping screened, preferred, aspirational, template, and adoption pathways.

docs.title: Honest Paper Positioning
docs.text:
The strongest paper right now is an empirical posting-content restructuring paper with a measurement contribution. It can credibly claim that AI/tool/platform/workflow language expanded across software-producing technical work and that this expansion appears within returning firms, across metros, and across SWE plus adjacent occupations.

It should not claim that AI eliminated junior SWE roles, that postings measure employment, that requirements are necessarily screened hiring bars, or that employer AI requirements outpace worker usage. Those stronger claims require the missing T19/T20/T22/T23-style evidence and interview validation.

The defensible contribution is narrower but still publishable: a transparent longitudinal posting dataset and a careful measurement framework showing how AI-era requirements diffused through technical job ads while seniority labels became unreliable anchors for interpreting junior and senior restructuring.

docs.title: Headline Finding
docs.text: The current data/unified.parquet artifact contains 1,397,647 rows total and 1,256,960 LinkedIn rows. After the default source_platform = 'linkedin', is_english = true, date_flag = 'ok' filters, the cross-source LinkedIn analysis frame has 1,211,512 rows, including 63,701 SWE rows. The binding cross-period constraints are not row volume; they are source-specific field availability, especially LLM-cleaned text coverage, company metadata gaps, asaniczka's native seniority gap, and geography/search-field semantics.

docs.text: All counts and coverage metrics were queried from data/unified.parquet with DuckDB via ./.venv/bin/python. Column coverage uses the default LinkedIn/English/date-ok analysis frame unless explicitly labeled as raw artifact counts. Non-null rate is count(column) / count(*); distinct count is exact COUNT(DISTINCT column) within each source and is_swe subset. The coverage CSV also includes is_swe=true and is_swe=false rows.

docs.title: Different Semantics Across Sources
docs.text:
• seniority_native: arshkon and scraped include entry/intern labels; asaniczka has no native entry-level labels and only contributes associate/mid-senior for the T02 comparison.
  • company_industry: arshkon has companion-data industry labels and scraped LinkedIn has platform industry labels; asaniczka and scraped Indeed have no usable industry coverage. Treat arshkon-vs-scraped industry comparisons as instrument-dependent.
  • company_size: arshkon uses Kaggle companion company fields, Indeed has parsed size coverage, and asaniczka/scraped LinkedIn are effectively absent. It is not a cross-period LinkedIn control.
  • date_posted: Kaggle sources are historical snapshot dates; scraped LinkedIn date_posted is sparse by design, while scrape_date defines the current observation window. Do not interpret date fields as a continuous unified event-time panel.
  • search_query and search_metro_*: for scraped rows they are scraper search metadata; for asaniczka, search_query comes from search_position; for arshkon it is absent. Use metro_area for posting geography, not search metadata.
  • is_remote: 2024 source remote flags are effectively an artifact. Use remote fields only as source-specific descriptors unless a downstream task explicitly validates them.
  • skills_raw / asaniczka_skills: arshkon/scraped skills fields and asaniczka structured skills come from different source instruments; compare them only as validation or source-specific artifacts, not as one pooled variable.
  • LLM columns (description_core_llm, swe_classification_llm, ghost_assessment_llm, yoe_min_years_llm) are coverage-managed sample outputs, not complete source fields. Always state coverage and denominator.

docs.text: category binding_constraint severity Text Cleaned text is LLM-only; scraped LinkedIn SWE labeled coverage is materially lower than historical sources. High Seniority Asaniczka has no native entry labels; seniority_final entry cells are small and partly LLM-derived. High Geography metro_area is the analysis field; search-metro fields exist only for scraped/search sources and multi-location rows can be unresolved. Moderate Company Company names/canonical names are strong, but industry and size are not consistently available across sources. Moderate-High Requirements YOE is broadly available but extracted from text; ghost/LLM requirement fields are coverage-limited and must be denominator-reported. Moderate

docs.title: Surprises & Unexpected Patterns
docs.text:
• Arshkon has near-complete LLM extraction coverage for SWE rows, while scraped LinkedIn has many not_selected SWE rows. This makes unbalanced cleaned-text comparisons risky even when raw descriptions are complete.
  • The raw artifact includes a large non-SWE/other reservoir in every source; Wave 2 tasks should not assume the file is already only SWE/adjacent/control.
  • seniority_final is complete by construction, but entry-level effective counts are much smaller than overall SWE counts, so the seniority problem is power and label construction rather than missingness.
  • Some fields with tempting face validity, especially remote status, company size, and search metadata, are source-instrument fields rather than comparable labor-market measures.

docs.title: Action Items For Downstream Agents
docs.text:
• Use seniority_final for primary seniority, but validate junior claims with the T30 panel and YOE proxies.
  • For text-sensitive work, restrict to llm_extraction_coverage = 'labeled' and report the source-specific denominator; do not silently backfill raw text.
  • For company controls, rely on company_name_effective / company_name_canonical; avoid pooled industry or size controls unless source-specific.
  • For geography, use metro_area and report unresolved/multi-location exclusions.

docs.title: T02. Asaniczka associate As A Junior Proxy
Plain Verdict
docs.text: Asaniczka seniority_native = 'associate' is not usable as a junior proxy under the dispatch rule. It matches arshkon entry on 1 of 4 required signals; the threshold is at least 3 of 4. Downstream analyses should not pool asaniczka native associate as an entry-level baseline. Use T30's J2 (seniority_final IN ('entry','associate')) or YOE-based junior variants instead.

docs.title: Methodology
docs.text:
Subset for all comparability metrics: source_platform = 'linkedin', is_english = true, date_flag = 'ok', is_swe = true, and the native-label groups named in each row. Top-title Jaccard uses each group's top 50 title_normalized values by row count. Explicit title-cue rates use raw lowercased title because title_normalized strips level indicators. Junior title-cue regex excludes the word associate to avoid circularly validating the tested label. YOE distribution denominators exclude rows with null yoe_extracted, and the known-YOE share is reported.

docs.title: Comparability Interpretation
docs.text:
• Top-title overlap is a weak signal because generic titles dominate every group; it is still reported because the task requires it.
  • The title-cue signal argues against substitution: asaniczka native associate has a much higher senior-cue rate than arshkon native entry and is nearest to arshkon native associate on the two-cue profile.
  • The YOE signal is denominator-limited because only rows with parsed YOE enter the distribution, but the known-YOE share is high enough for a directional comparison.
  • The seniority_final distribution is decisive for the substitution question: only a small fraction of asaniczka native associate rows become entry under the combined rule/LLM seniority column.

docs.title: Surprises & Unexpected Patterns
docs.text:
• Arshkon native entry still maps mostly to seniority_final = 'unknown', not to entry; this confirms that production seniority intentionally requires explicit title/LLM evidence and does not simply mirror native labels.
  • Asaniczka native associate looks less junior than the label might imply: its explicit junior-title cue rate is low and its seniority_final distribution is dominated by unknown/mid-senior rather than entry.
  • Native labels and production labels are measuring different objects. Native entry is useful as an arshkon diagnostic, but seniority_final is more conservative and creates smaller effective junior cells.

docs.title: Action Items For Downstream Agents
docs.text:
• Do not use asaniczka native associate as a stand-alone junior baseline.
  • Report arshkon-only native-entry checks separately from seniority_final analyses.
  • Use T30 J1-J4/J5-J6 panels for junior claims and include YOE-known denominators for YOE variants.

docs.title: Headline
docs.text:
seniority_final is defensible as the production seniority column, but it is conservative. The labels that are assigned show strong YOE ordering, and known-only agreement with LinkedIn native labels is moderate to substantial. The main failure mode is not random mislabeling; it is under-labeling into unknown, especially for scraped rows and for L2/L3/L4/L5-style title codes.

Evidence base: existing T03 tables in exploration/tables/T03/. No data was re-queried for this report. All source counts use the exploration default filter: LinkedIn rows, English rows, date_flag = 'ok', and is_swe = true.

docs.title: Native Label Agreement
docs.text:
Native labels are a diagnostic, not the primary label. The all-values comparison is low because seniority_final intentionally leaves many rows as unknown, while native labels often force a class.

Comparison Source group n Exact match Cohen's kappa Native vs final, all final values arshkon 2024 3,283 44.3% 0.175 Native vs final, all final values scraped 2026 34,839 41.2% 0.112 Native intern->entry and executive->director, all final values arshkon 2024 3,283 45.0% 0.184 Native intern->entry and executive->director, all final values scraped 2026 34,839 42.8% 0.135 Same mapping, final known only arshkon 2024 1,667 88.5% 0.454 Same mapping, final known only scraped 2026 15,982 93.3% 0.646

Known-only agreement is much stronger, especially in scraped 2026. That pattern supports the design choice: high precision where the pipeline assigns a label, with a large residual unknown class rather than forced labels.

Per-class agreement against native labels is uneven:

Source group Native class Native n Exact match rate arshkon 2024 entry 738 7.2% arshkon 2024 associate 263 1.5% arshkon 2024 mid-senior 2,231 62.4% scraped 2026 entry 4,689 10.2% scraped 2026 associate 1,405 0.5% scraped 2026 mid-senior 27,759 49.9%

This is mostly an unknown issue. For example, 618 of arshkon's 738 native-entry rows and 3,986 of scraped 2026's 4,689 native-entry rows are unknown under seniority_final.

docs.title: Native-Label YOE Diagnostic
docs.text:
YOE evidence favors seniority_final over native labels for identifying actual entry-level rows.

Source group Label system Label n YOE known Median YOE Share YOE <= 2 Share YOE >= 5 arshkon 2024 final entry 175 37.1% 2.0 78.5% 6.2% arshkon 2024 native entry 738 62.1% 3.0 28.6% 42.6% scraped 2026 final entry 1,275 24.9% 2.0 81.8% 6.3% scraped 2026 native entry 4,689 38.8% 2.0 81.1% 10.6% arshkon 2024 final mid-senior 2,064 67.6% 5.0 6.3% 74.6% scraped 2026 final mid-senior 17,418 75.4% 5.0 7.8% 72.9% arshkon 2024 final unknown 2,424 58.8% 4.0 20.2% 48.6% scraped 2026 final unknown 21,841 63.6% 4.0 24.7% 44.3%

The final-entry class has the expected low-YOE profile in both periods. Native entry in arshkon is much noisier, with 42.6% of YOE-known rows requiring 5+ years. This is a strong warning against using native labels as a pooled baseline and supports the pipeline's conservative relabeling.

The unknown pool is substantive, with median YOE around 4 years and many 5+ YOE rows. Unknowns are not "junior by default" and should be reported separately or handled through the T30 panel.

docs.title: LLM Weak-Marker Spot Check
docs.text:
The weak-marker population is small: 21 SWE rows where seniority_final_source = 'llm' and the title contains weak level markers such as L2, L3, L4, L5, roman numerals, or level 3.

Population n Sample n Heuristic consistent Heuristic inconsistent Inconsistent share 21 21 2 19 90.5%

The inconsistent cases are mostly titles like Software Engineer L3, L2 Data Engineer, Software Engineer L5, and ServiceNow Developer (L4) where the heuristic expected associate/mid-senior but the LLM result was often unknown. Because the expected label is heuristic and the population is only 21 rows, this is not a formal error-rate estimate. It is a clear blind spot for company-level code markers.

Interpretation: this failure mode is conservative. It suppresses known-seniority coverage rather than inflating entry or senior labels, but it can attenuate senior/junior shares for employers that use explicit level codes.

docs.title: Defensibility Recommendation
docs.text:
Use seniority_final as the production seniority column. It is not a high-recall seniority classifier, but the assigned labels are much more YOE-coherent than native labels, and known-only native agreement is acceptable for an exploratory seniority instrument.

Required constraints for downstream work:

  • Report unknown seniority rather than dropping it silently.
  • Use the T30 panel for every seniority-stratified finding.
  • Validate entry-level findings with YOE proxies (J3/J4) because label-only entry has coverage limits.
  • Do not use pooled seniority_native for junior baselines. Asaniczka has no native entry-level labels by construction, and arshkon native entry is YOE-noisy.
  • Treat L2/L3/L4/L5 title-code employers as a known under-labeling risk.

docs.title: Surprises & Unexpected Patterns
docs.text:
• The most surprising result is that arshkon native-entry rows look much less junior by YOE than seniority_final entry rows.
  • Scraped 2026 has a much larger unknown seniority share than either historical source, even though it also has native LinkedIn labels for many rows.
  • The LLM weak-marker check failed mostly on title level codes rather than on vague responsibility language. A small, well-tested level-code rule could recover coverage, but only after employer-specific ambiguity is handled.
  • associate under seniority_final is tiny, and its YOE profile is closer to junior/mid than to classic senior. It should not be assumed equivalent to asaniczka native associate.

docs.title: Headline
docs.text:
The SWE flag is usable for Wave 1/Gate 1, with one important sensitivity: title_lookup_llm is the fragile tier. The full SWE sample is dominated by regex matches (91.2%), while the title lookup tier is only 7.2% overall but carries most of the visible false-positive risk. False negatives remain visible among unresolved titles like Ruby on Rails Developer, ETL Developer, and Software Integration Engineer.

Evidence base: existing T04 tables in exploration/tables/T04/. Manual assessments below use the two existing 50-row sample CSVs only. No full data rerun was performed.

docs.title: SWE Tier Breakdown
docs.text:
Overall SWE rows under the default LinkedIn/English/date-ok filter:

SWE classification tier n Share regex 58,064 91.2% title_lookup_llm 4,583 7.2% embedding_high 1,054 1.7% total 63,701 100.0%

By source-period:

Source-period SWE n Regex Title lookup Embedding high kaggle_arshkon 2024-04 4,691 88.6% 9.7% 1.6% kaggle_asaniczka 2024-01 18,129 87.0% 10.9% 2.1% scraped 2026-03 19,777 93.4% 5.1% 1.5% scraped 2026-04 21,104 93.2% 5.4% 1.4%

The historical sources rely more heavily on title lookup than scraped 2026, so title-lookup false positives can create source-period bias.

docs.title: Borderline SWE Manual-Sample Assessment
docs.text:
The borderline SWE sample is 50 rows from is_swe = true where either swe_confidence is 0.3-0.7 or swe_classification_tier = 'title_lookup_llm'. In the current artifact, all sampled rows are title_lookup_llm with confidence 0.9.

Manual assessment from title plus excerpt:

  • Likely primary SWE or software-test/development roles: about 38/50.
  • Likely false positives or SWE-adjacent/non-SWE roles: about 12/50.
  • Main false-positive families: generic Test Engineer, Reliability Engineer, Automation Engineer, Quality Assurance Engineer, and Quality Engineer in aerospace, electronics, manufacturing, medical-device, carwash/equipment, or field reliability contexts.
  • Clear true positives in the same tier: ReactJS Developer, Angular developer, AI Developer, DevSecOps Engineer, Firmware Engineer, Java Architect, UI Developer, Software Engineering Manager, and software QA/test automation roles.

Estimated precision for this deliberately fragile tier is roughly 76% in the sample. Since title_lookup_llm is 7.2% of all SWE rows, the rough full-SWE false-positive contribution is about 1.7 percentage points if regex and embedding-high tiers are assumed high precision. This is not a full-population false-positive rate.

docs.title: Borderline Non-SWE Manual-Sample Assessment
docs.text:
The borderline non-SWE population is is_swe = false with title-normalized matches on engineer, developer, or software. It is large: 97,786 rows across the four source-periods in the existing population table.

Manual assessment of the existing 50-row sample:

  • Likely missed primary SWE: about 6/50.
  • Additional SWE-adjacent or ambiguous technical misses: about 6/50.
  • Clear false negatives include Ruby on Rails Developer, Principal Engineer, YouTube ML Recommendations Systems, ETL Developer, Software Integration Engineer, and probably some BI/data/reporting developer roles.
  • Correct non-SWE exclusions dominate the sample: civil, structural, mechanical, electrical, industrial, geotechnical, project, process, field service, data-center facilities, and manufacturing engineering roles.

This sample is enriched for hard cases and should not be converted into a dataset-wide false-negative rate. It does show that unresolved developer/software titles need a sensitivity check when downstream analyses depend on high recall.

docs.title: Adjacent and Control Profile
docs.text:
Overall classification group totals:

Group n SWE 63,701 SWE-adjacent 23,408 Control 155,745 Other non-SWE/no flag 968,658

Top SWE-adjacent titles are conceptually close but distinct from primary SWE: quality engineer, data scientist, network engineer, data analyst, architect, solutions architect, security engineer, systems administrator, data architect, and database administrator.

Top control titles look distinct from SWE: accountant, registered nurse, electrical engineer, financial analyst, mechanical engineer, accounting manager, civil engineer, and nurse variants. The control group is broad and should not be interpreted as a single occupation family.

docs.title: Dual-Flag Check
docs.text:
Dual-flag violations are zero.

Default-filter rows Dual-flag violations No-flag rows 1,211,512 0 968,658

The flags are mutually exclusive in the existing artifact.

docs.title: Boundary Cases
docs.text:
Aggregated boundary-case profile:

Boundary case Total n SWE SWE-adjacent Control Other/no flag generic software engineer 39,503 33,289 675 9 5,530 devops/sre/platform 5,331 5,177 23 0 131 data engineer 4,312 4,311 1 0 0 qa/test/sdet 4,101 941 482 30 2,648 ml engineer 3,351 3,342 4 0 5 sales/solutions engineer 2,160 22 78 3 2,057 support engineer 859 0 45 4 810

ML engineer, data engineer, and DevOps/SRE/platform roles are classified consistently as SWE. Sales/solutions engineer and support engineer are mostly excluded, which is desirable.

The unstable boundary is QA/test/SDET: most rows are neither SWE nor adjacent, but a meaningful subset is SWE or adjacent. This boundary can vary by source and employer wording. Generic software engineer also has a surprisingly large no-flag remainder, which should be checked before any high-recall SWE sensitivity.

docs.title: False-Positive and False-Negative Estimates
docs.text:
Best estimate from existing samples:

  • False positives: approximately 24% within the title-lookup borderline SWE sample, concentrated in test/reliability/automation/quality roles outside software. Because title lookup is only 7.2% of SWE, this implies a rough full-SWE FP contribution around 1.5-2.0 percentage points under high-precision assumptions for other tiers.
  • False negatives: approximately 12% primary-SWE misses in the enriched borderline non-SWE sample, or up to 24% if SWE-adjacent/ambiguous technical roles are included. This is not a dataset-wide FNR because the sample intentionally selects titles with engineer, developer, or software.

Recommended sensitivity for downstream tasks: rerun key findings excluding swe_classification_tier = 'title_lookup_llm'. For high-recall checks, separately profile unresolved developer, software, ETL, BI, integration, and ML recommendations titles.

docs.title: Surprises & Unexpected Patterns
docs.text:
• The most fragile positive tier is smaller in 2026 than in 2024, so false positives may bias historical/current comparisons if ignored.
  • QA/test roles are the hardest occupational boundary. Some are software automation roles; others are hardware, manufacturing, or compliance test roles.
  • Several obvious software titles appear in the borderline non-SWE sample. This suggests recall risk in the unresolved pool, not just precision risk in the SWE pool.
  • Data engineer and ML engineer classification is very stable, which is useful because those roles often sit near the SWE/SWE-adjacent boundary.

docs.title: Headline Finding
docs.text:
The three LinkedIn datasets are not interchangeable instruments. The 2026 scraped LinkedIn corpus is longer, geographically different, and has a much larger title vocabulary than either 2024 Kaggle source. Company and title overlap are low enough that source composition can easily masquerade as temporal change.

The strongest comparability result is narrow: seniority_final distributions among known-seniority SWE rows are more similar between arshkon and scraped LinkedIn than between the two 2024 Kaggle sources. That does not make seniority solved, because unknown-seniority rates remain high and asaniczka has no native entry labels by construction.

docs.title: Methodology
docs.text:
Subset: LinkedIn-only SWE postings with is_english = true, date_flag = 'ok', and is_swe = true. The analysis used description_length, company_name_canonical, state_normalized, seniority_final, title_normalized, and company_industry.

Sample sizes:

Source SWE rows Company known Seniority known YOE known arshkon 4,691 4,646 2,267 2,911 asaniczka 18,129 18,129 11,746 12,834 scraped LinkedIn 40,881 40,881 19,040 27,581

Source artifacts: exploration/tables/T05/*.csv and figures in exploration/figures/T05/.

docs.title: Description Length
docs.text:
Scraped LinkedIn descriptions are much longer than both 2024 sources.

Pair Mean A Mean B Median A Median B KS statistic arshkon vs asaniczka 3,306 3,881 2,974 3,733 0.144 arshkon vs scraped LinkedIn 3,306 4,912 2,974 4,862 0.296 asaniczka vs scraped LinkedIn 3,881 4,912 3,733 4,862 0.198

The arshkon-to-scraped length shift is about 2.05x the within-2024 arshkon-vs-asaniczka KS gap; the asaniczka-to-scraped shift is only 1.38x. Treat length increases as real enough to control for, but not cleanly separable from collection-format differences such as scraped markdown, bullet structure, and richer live posting text.

docs.title: Company Overlap
docs.text:
Company overlap is low across all pairs.

Pair Companies A Companies B Intersection Jaccard arshkon vs asaniczka 2,298 4,948 742 0.114 arshkon vs scraped LinkedIn 2,298 7,940 1,004 0.109 asaniczka vs scraped LinkedIn 4,948 7,940 1,529 0.135

Top-50 overlap is somewhat better: arshkon and scraped LinkedIn share 21 of their top-50 employers, versus 13 for arshkon/asaniczka and 15 for asaniczka/scraped. This helps for large-employer robustness checks, but it does not support treating full-corpus company composition as stable.

docs.title: Geography
docs.text:
State shares differ materially. Cramer's V is 0.137 for arshkon vs asaniczka, 0.271 for arshkon vs scraped LinkedIn, and 0.346 for asaniczka vs scraped LinkedIn. State exclusions also differ: 25.0% of arshkon SWE rows, 8.4% of asaniczka rows, and 12.9% of scraped LinkedIn rows lack state after multi-location and parsing exclusions.

Geographic comparisons are therefore partly instrument comparisons. Scraped data comes from the project search frame; Kaggle snapshots are broader LinkedIn artifacts with different missingness.

docs.title: Seniority
docs.text:
Unknown seniority is large: 51.7% in arshkon, 35.2% in asaniczka, and 53.4% in scraped LinkedIn. Among known-seniority rows:

Source Entry Associate Mid-Senior Director arshkon 7.7% 0.7% 91.0% 0.6% asaniczka 1.7% 0.2% 97.3% 0.8% scraped LinkedIn 6.7% 0.3% 91.5% 1.5%

Pairwise seniority Cramer's V is 0.035 for arshkon vs scraped LinkedIn, much smaller than the 0.140 within-2024 arshkon-vs-asaniczka difference. This is the one comparability dimension where arshkon looks like a better 2024 baseline than pooled 2024.

docs.title: Title Vocabulary
docs.text:
Title-set overlap is very low: Jaccard is 0.048 for arshkon vs asaniczka, 0.026 for arshkon vs scraped LinkedIn, and 0.037 for asaniczka vs scraped LinkedIn. The title-space distance is almost entirely within the range of cross-source instrument noise.

The unique-title lists show both collection artifacts and real content drift. 2024-only high-frequency titles include highly specific full-stack and security-clearance variants; 2026-only titles include AI platform, AI research/evaluation, agentic intelligence, cloud infrastructure, DevOps, and remote variants.

docs.title: Industry
docs.text:
Industry is only comparable for arshkon and scraped LinkedIn; asaniczka lacks industry data. The arshkon-vs-scraped industry distribution differs strongly (Cramer's V = 0.292).

Top shares:

Industry arshkon scraped LinkedIn IT Services and IT Consulting 25.8% 12.5% Software Development 13.1% 26.0% Financial Services 8.4% 7.2% Staffing and Recruiting 5.8% 2.6% Defense and Space Manufacturing 2.6% 3.9%

Industry composition is a major confound for temporal analysis. Downstream models should include industry where available and avoid interpreting arshkon-vs-scraped industry shifts as labor-market shifts without source controls.

docs.title: Artifact Assessment
docs.text:
Most trustworthy cross-period comparisons:

Metric Assessment seniority_final known distribution Best arshkon-vs-scraped comparability, but unknown rates are high and T30 J1-J4 sensitivity is still required. Description length Real difference likely exists, but magnitude is format-sensitive. Use length controls and within-2024 calibration. State distribution Useful for describing frame differences, not clean temporal change. Company overlap Low overlap; use company fixed effects, caps, or matched-company panels for employer-sensitive claims. Title vocabulary Highly instrument-sensitive. Do not use raw title uniqueness as a temporal claim without normalization and employer controls. Industry arshkon vs scraped only; no within-2024 calibration because asaniczka industry is missing.

docs.title: Within-2024 Calibration
docs.text: Metric Within-2024 effect arshkon vs scraped Signal/noise Verdict Description length KS 0.144 0.296 2.05 Above noise only for arshkon baseline. Company Jaccard distance 0.886 0.891 1.01 Not above instrument noise. State-share Cramer's V 0.137 0.271 1.98 Borderline to above noise. Seniority Cramer's V 0.140 0.035 0.25 Cross-period difference smaller than within-2024 noise. Title Jaccard distance 0.952 0.974 1.02 Not above instrument noise. Industry Cramer's V N/A 0.292 N/A No asaniczka industry calibration.

docs.title: Platform Labeling Stability
docs.text:
The top 20 common titles appear in both arshkon and scraped LinkedIn. Native seniority labels vary by title: software engineer has a small native-label difference (Cramer's V = 0.067), while android developer, developer, and java developer show large differences.

YOE checks show mixed mechanisms. For software engineer mid-senior cells, median YOE is stable at 5 years in both periods. For several entry-labeled title cells, scraped LinkedIn has lower YOE than arshkon, such as java developer entry median YOE 5 vs 2 and software developer entry median 3 vs 2. That pattern is more consistent with title/cell composition changes than a single platform relabeling artifact.

docs.title: Indeed Cross-Validation
docs.text:
Indeed scraped rows provide a platform sensitivity check, but seniority coverage is narrow because Indeed has no native seniority and relies on strong-rule labels.

Indeed SWE sample: 11,860 rows. Seniority-known rows: 5,478. Entry share of known seniority: 6.6%. YOE-known rows: 8,365. YOE<=2 share of YOE-known rows: 18.1%.

This broadly matches scraped LinkedIn's nonzero junior signal (seniority_final known-entry share 6.7%; T30 J3 scraped share-of-known 17.2%). The cross-platform check weakens a pure LinkedIn-labeling-artifact explanation, but it does not solve unknown-seniority or source-composition problems.

docs.title: Surprises & Unexpected Patterns
docs.text:
• Arshkon and scraped LinkedIn are more similar on known-seniority distribution than arshkon and asaniczka are, despite being different periods.
  • Top-50 employer overlap is highest for arshkon vs scraped LinkedIn, not for the two 2024 Kaggle sources.
  • Title vocabulary is so sparse that raw title-set overlap is mostly an instrument diagnostic, not a market-change measure.
  • Indeed's aggregate entry signal is close to scraped LinkedIn despite Indeed's much weaker seniority instrumentation.

docs.title: Downstream Action Items
docs.text:
• Use arshkon, not pooled 2024, as the main native-label sanity baseline for entry-level claims.
  • Report every junior/senior claim under the T30 panel; do not rely on one seniority definition.
  • Use within-2024 calibration whenever comparing 2024 vs 2026.
  • For text analyses, control for description length and use description_core_llm only when llm_extraction_coverage = 'labeled'.
  • Treat company, title, geography, and industry composition as first-order confounds, not background metadata.

docs.title: Headline Finding
docs.text:
Company concentration is moderate in row-share terms but high enough to distort any corpus-level analysis. The 2026 scraped LinkedIn source has 7,940 distinct known companies, yet the top 20 companies contribute 18.1% of known-company SWE postings and the top 50 contribute 26.9%. Entry-level posting is much more concentrated than posting volume: among scraped LinkedIn companies with at least 5 SWE postings, 79.4% have zero J1 entry-labeled rows and 53.1% have zero YOE<=2 rows.

The strongest Gate 1 warning is not only "cap prolific firms." It is "do not interpret entry-share, text-frequency, topic, or network results until entry-specialist employers, aggregators, duplicate templates, and company composition have been handled explicitly."

docs.text:
Completed from existing partial artifacts:

  • analysis_sample_counts.csv
  • concentration_metrics.csv
  • top20_employer_profile.csv
  • figures/T06/top_company_shares.png

Recomputed under memory limits:

  • duplicate_template_top10_by_source.csv

The recomputation used exploration/scripts/T06_generate_missing_sql.py with DuckDB only: PRAGMA memory_limit='4GB'; PRAGMA threads=1;. It did not use pandas, did not call LLMs, and did not run preprocessing stages. The existing pandas-heavy T06 script was not rerun.

docs.title: Methodology
docs.text:
Subset: all SWE postings across all source-platforms with is_english = true, date_flag = 'ok', and is_swe = true.

Company grouping: company_name_canonical, excluding missing company for company-level metrics.

Source keys:

Source key SWE rows Known-company rows Distinct companies kaggle_arshkon 4,691 4,646 2,298 kaggle_asaniczka 18,129 18,129 4,948 scraped_linkedin 40,881 40,881 7,940 scraped_indeed 11,860 11,511 3,308

Entry variants follow the T30 panel definitions: J1 = seniority_final = 'entry', J2 = seniority_final IN ('entry','associate'), J3 = yoe_extracted <= 2, and J4 = yoe_extracted <= 3. The T30 aggregate panel was loaded as a reference; row-level company membership was recomputed in DuckDB from those definitions because the panel CSV is aggregate, not row-level.

AI mention prevalence uses a raw-description binary pattern covering AI, ML, LLM, generative AI, GPT, ChatGPT, OpenAI, Anthropic, Claude, Copilot, Cursor, RAG, agentic, AI agent, and prompt engineering. Tech count uses the keyword list in the SQL script and should be treated as a coarse concentration diagnostic, not a validated skills measure.

docs.title: Concentration Metrics
docs.text:
Source key HHI Gini Top 1 Top 5 Top 20 Top 50 kaggle_arshkon 0.0016 0.433 1.1% 4.6% 12.3% 20.9% kaggle_asaniczka 0.0054 0.641 5.0% 12.2% 21.1% 30.6% scraped_linkedin 0.0036 0.693 4.2% 9.4% 18.1% 26.9% scraped_indeed 0.0077 0.642 4.6% 16.7% 28.3% 38.4%

Aggregator exclusion lowers concentration in the historical asaniczka and scraped LinkedIn sources. For scraped LinkedIn, top-20 share falls from 18.1% to 16.6%; for asaniczka it falls from 21.1% to 14.1%. Indeed is still concentrated after aggregator exclusion because its dominant firms are mostly direct employers.

docs.title: Top-20 Employer Profile
docs.text:
The top employer profiles show different source shapes:

  • arshkon is comparatively diffuse. Its top companies are Apex Systems and Capital One at 52 postings each, or about 1.1% of SWE rows apiece.
  • asaniczka is dominated by ClearanceJobs (898 postings, 5.0%) and Capital One (658, 3.6%).
  • scraped LinkedIn is led by Jobs via Dice (1,711 postings, 4.2%), Google (757, 1.9%), and JPMorgan Chase (657, 1.6%).
  • scraped Indeed is led by Apple (532 postings, 4.5%), JPMorgan Chase (411, 3.5%), Amazon (409, 3.4%), and Google (361, 3.0%).

The top-20 table also shows why row-weighted text analyses are risky. In scraped LinkedIn, Google, Anduril, NVIDIA, Microsoft, JPMorgan, and Jobs via Dice have very different AI-mention rates, YOE profiles, and description lengths. A term-frequency or AI-rate result can easily become a large-employer mix result.

docs.title: Duplicate-Template Audit
docs.text:
Stage 4 multi-location collapse removed the worst expected exact-template duplication, but residual exact duplicates remain.

Top residual examples:

Source key Employer Postings Distinct hashes Largest exact-template share kaggle_arshkon SynergisticIT 5 1 100.0% kaggle_asaniczka Revature 9 4 33.3% scraped_linkedin Intellibus 8 2 87.5% scraped_linkedin DataAnnotation 71 26 53.5% scraped_indeed Compass Group USA 5 4 40.0%

This is now a verification finding, not the old multi-location failure mode. The remaining issue is exact or near-exact reuse across titles, companies, or posting variants that are not collapsed by the Stage 4 key.

docs.title: Entry Posting Concentration
docs.text:
Source key Companies Any J1 entry Any YOE<=2 >=5 companies zero J1 >=5 companies zero YOE<=2 kaggle_arshkon 2,298 6.4% 13.9% 79.3% 56.7% kaggle_asaniczka 4,948 2.9% 15.8% 88.9% 58.1% scraped_linkedin 7,940 8.4% 19.6% 79.4% 53.1% scraped_indeed 3,308 7.9% 18.8% 80.8% 45.3%

Among companies that do post entry roles, the distribution is polarized. In scraped LinkedIn, the median J1 share among J1-posting companies is 33.3% of own postings and the 90th percentile is 100%. For YOE<=2 posters, the median share is 50% of YOE-known postings and the 90th percentile is 100%.

Interpretation: entry-level posting is a specialized employer activity. Aggregate entry-share trends are not market-wide unless they survive company-weighting and exclusion of entry-specialist employers.

docs.title: Within vs Between Decomposition
docs.text:
Overlap panel: 125 companies have at least 5 SWE postings in both arshkon and scraped LinkedIn. YOE-based variants use 121 companies after requiring YOE denominators.

Metric Full change Common-panel change Within component Between/reweighting component Noncommon residual Entry J1 -0.005 -0.003 -0.006 +0.004 -0.003 Entry J2 -0.007 -0.004 -0.009 +0.004 -0.003 Entry J3 +0.022 +0.073 +0.004 +0.069 -0.051 Entry J4 +0.027 +0.121 +0.031 +0.090 -0.094 AI mention prevalence +0.352 +0.395 +0.283 +0.112 -0.044 Mean description length +1,595 chars +1,766 chars +963 chars +803 chars -171 chars Mean tech count +0.819 +0.666 +0.865 -0.199 +0.153

The seniority story changes by junior definition. J1/J2 entry shares decline slightly, while J3/J4 YOE-based junior shares rise, especially in the common-company panel. Most of the J3/J4 common-panel increase is between-company reweighting, not within-company change. This is a required Gate 1 constraint for any "junior share rose/fell" claim.

AI mentions and description length rise strongly within overlapping companies, so those shifts are not explained only by employer turnover. Tech count rises mostly within companies, with between-company reweighting partly offsetting the increase.

docs.title: Entry-Specialist Employers
docs.text:
Shared artifact written: exploration/artifacts/shared/entry_specialist_employers.csv.

The artifact flags every company-source cell with at least 5 SWE postings where any J1-J4 variant gives a junior share above 60%. It contains 308 flagged rows, including 196 scraped LinkedIn rows. The top 20 were manually reviewed into the requested employer-type categories.

Category cross-tab:

Category Aggregator? Flagged companies Flagged postings bulk-posting consulting false 17 158 bulk-posting consulting true 7 84 college-jobsite intermediary false 1 20 college-jobsite intermediary true 1 6 direct employer false 248 3,315 direct employer true 13 312 staffing firm false 9 57 tech-giant intern pipeline false 11 2,180 tech-giant intern pipeline true 1 335

Important caveat: the requested flag rule is intentionally broad. Many J3/J4 flags are driven by small yoe_known_n denominators, including companies with only 1 to 5 YOE-known postings. The artifact includes yoe_known_n, known_seniority_n, and each variant share so downstream tasks can run a stricter sensitivity, such as requiring yoe_known_n >= 5 for YOE-based specialist flags.

docs.title: Aggregator Profile
docs.text:
Aggregator shares differ strongly:

Source key Aggregator share kaggle_arshkon 9.2% kaggle_asaniczka 16.2% scraped_linkedin 16.6% scraped_indeed 2.1%

Aggregators also differ systematically. In scraped LinkedIn, aggregator descriptions are shorter on average (3,905 vs 5,113 chars), have higher unknown-seniority share (65.8% vs 51.0%), lower AI mention prevalence (43.3% vs 52.6%), and slightly higher mean tech count (5.41 vs 5.07). Their known-seniority entry share is higher than non-aggregators (8.25% vs 6.48%), but their all-posting J1 share is lower because unknown seniority is more common.

Conclusion: aggregator exclusion is mandatory for entry, seniority, and text analyses. Aggregators are not simply "more AI" or "longer descriptions"; they change the composition in multiple directions.

docs.title: New Entrants
docs.text:
New entrants are 2026 scraped companies with no canonical company match in either 2024 Kaggle source.

Source key New entrant? Companies Postings Posting share Entry known share YOE<=2 share AI mention share Mean length scraped_linkedin false 2,024 22,564 55.2% 6.2% 19.1% 51.1% 5,142 scraped_linkedin true 5,916 18,317 44.8% 7.4% 14.6% 51.1% 4,629 scraped_indeed false 749 5,749 49.9% 4.5% 21.0% 53.0% 5,817 scraped_indeed true 2,559 5,762 50.1% 8.7% 14.8% 47.6% 5,073

Scraped LinkedIn new entrants are almost half the current corpus by postings. They are not more AI-heavy than returning companies, but they are shorter and have lower YOE<=2 shares despite slightly higher known-entry share. This again separates seniority-label behavior from YOE behavior.

docs.title: Concentration Prediction Table
docs.text:
Analysis category Risk Recommended default Entry share High Report J1-J4, add company-weighted estimates, and exclude entry_specialist_employers.csv as a required sensitivity. AI mention rate Medium Use row-level rates plus company-clustered or company-weighted sensitivity; cap prolific firms for corpus summaries. Description length Medium Use row-level distribution tests, then report company-weighted mean/median and aggregator-excluded sensitivity. Term frequencies High Cap at 20-50 postings per company and deduplicate exact description_hash within company before term ranking. Topic models High Deduplicate exact templates, cap per company, and inspect topic employer concentration before interpretation. Co-occurrence networks High Build company-capped networks and require terms/edges to appear across at least 20 distinct companies.

The CSV version is exploration/tables/T06/concentration_prediction_table.csv.

docs.title: Surprises & Unexpected Patterns
docs.text:
• Exact-template duplication is not zero after the Stage 4 fix; scraped LinkedIn still has employers with large residual exact-template shares.
  • J1/J2 and J3/J4 tell opposite stories in the matched-company decomposition. Label-based entry declines slightly, while YOE-based junior shares rise.
  • New scraped LinkedIn entrants are not more AI-heavy than returning employers; AI mention prevalence is essentially identical.
  • Aggregators in scraped LinkedIn are shorter and less AI-heavy than non-aggregators, contrary to the simple assumption that aggregators always inflate text and AI terms.
  • The entry-specialist artifact contains many apparent direct employers, partly because YOE-based variants can flag companies with very small YOE-known denominators.

docs.title: Gate 1 Constraints
docs.text:
• Do not report a single junior-share estimate. Report J1-J4 and explain disagreements.
  • Any aggregate entry-share result must include company-weighted and entry-specialist-excluded sensitivities.
  • Text-frequency, topic, and network analyses should be company-capped and exact-template deduplicated.
  • Aggregators must be reported separately or excluded as a required sensitivity.
  • New-entrant and returning-company profiles should be separated for source-composition-sensitive claims.

docs.title: Headline Finding
docs.text:
The main feasibility result is uneven by seniority definition. All-SWE comparisons and broad senior definitions are well powered. Junior analyses are power-bound if they use T30's label-quality primary J1 (seniority_final = entry) on arshkon alone: the arshkon-vs-scraped J1 MDE is 11.3 pp for binary outcomes and 0.226 SD for continuous outcomes. Power improves materially with YOE-based junior definitions: J3 is adequate arshkon-only and well powered when 2024 is pooled; J4 is well powered in both source specifications.

Power-only input for Gate 1: J4 is the strongest junior definition and J3 is the better powered stricter low-YOE validator. This does not overrule T30's label-quality recommendation of J1 as the clean employer-posted junior label. It means Wave 2/3 junior content analyses should treat J1 as a thin, high-label-quality estimand and pre-plan J3/J4 as powered checks. On the senior side, power and T30's label-quality recommendation align: S1 should be primary, paired with S4. S2 is not usable for inferential director-only analysis.

docs.title: Inputs And Filters
docs.text:
Local data filter for all sample-size, metro, company, state, and industry tables:

source_platform = 'linkedin'
AND is_english = true
AND date_flag = 'ok'
AND is_swe = true

Current default-filtered LinkedIn SWE rows:

source date range n kaggle_arshkon 2024-04-05 to 2024-04-20 4,691 kaggle_asaniczka 2024-01-12 to 2024-01-17 18,129 scraped 2026-03-19 to 2026-04-13 40,881

Seniority group sizes were loaded from T30's canonical panel at exploration/artifacts/shared/seniority_definition_panel.csv; all-SWE rows were queried locally from data/unified.parquet. MDEs are two-sample, two-sided alpha 0.05, 80% power. MDE_binary is a conservative absolute percentage-point MDE using worst-case variance at p = 0.5. MDE_continuous is a standardized mean difference.

Verdicts:

verdict rule well-powered binary MDE <= 5 pp and continuous MDE <= 0.10 SD adequate binary MDE <= 8 pp and continuous MDE <= 0.20 SD thin binary MDE <= 14 pp and continuous MDE <= 0.35 SD underpowered worse than thin, or a minimum cell below 50
