docs.title: Gate 1 Verdicts
docs.text:
All-SWE analyses can proceed. They are well powered under both arshkon-only and pooled-2024 historical baselines.

Junior analyses need a split decision. J1 is too thin for small effects in arshkon-only comparisons; it can detect only large binary differences around 11 pp or continuous differences around 0.23 SD. J2 adds little power over J1. J3 is the minimum practical powered junior definition for arshkon-only work, and J4 is the power-favorable junior definition. Because J3/J4 are YOE-based rather than employer-posted labels, use them as powered validators rather than silently redefining the junior construct.

Senior analyses should use S1 as primary from both power and label-quality perspectives. S4 is also well powered and should remain the YOE-based validator. S2 director-only is descriptive only; there are 13 arshkon director rows. S3 is also too thin and T30 already flagged it as a title-normalization artifact.

docs.title: Metro Feasibility
docs.text:
Full CSVs: metro_counts_by_period.csv, metro_feasibility_summary.csv, metro_exclusions.csv

Twenty-six metros have at least 50 SWE postings in both 2024 and 2026. Twenty-five have at least 100 in both periods. New York City passes the >=50 threshold but not >=100 because historical rows have n = 70. The 25 >=100 metros are:

San Francisco Bay Area; Washington DC Metro; Seattle Metro; Dallas-Fort Worth Metro; Salt Lake City Metro; Los Angeles Metro; Boston Metro; Austin Metro; Atlanta Metro; Charlotte Metro; Chicago Metro; Philadelphia Metro; Denver Metro; Phoenix Metro; Detroit Metro; Minneapolis-Saint Paul; Raleigh-Durham-Chapel Hill; Houston Metro; Columbus Metro; San Diego Metro; Pittsburgh Metro; Miami Metro; Tampa Bay Metro; Nashville Metro; Portland Metro.

Metro exclusions are large. metro_area IS NULL excludes 7,162 of 22,820 2024 rows and 10,321 of 40,881 2026 rows. Multi-location rows are a subset of that exclusion: 1,010 in 2024 and 3,090 in 2026. The remaining null-metro rows are not marked multi-location, so metro analyses should report the location-known frame explicitly.

docs.title: Company Panel Feasibility
docs.text:
Full CSVs: company_overlap_ge3.csv, company_overlap_summary.csv

There are 240 companies with at least 3 SWE postings in both arshkon and scraped. This panel covers 1,792 arshkon rows and 11,499 scraped rows. The average covered company has 7.5 arshkon rows and 47.9 scraped rows, so within-company analyses are feasible but strongly unbalanced toward 2026. Top overlapping employers include Google, JPMorgan Chase, Microsoft, Amazon, AWS, Booz Allen Hamilton, Wells Fargo, Capital One, Motion Recruitment, Actalent, and Anduril.

docs.title: BLS OEWS State Employment
docs.text:
State benchmark succeeded using official BLS OEWS time-series metadata and the BLS public API:

  • metadata: https://downloadt.bls.gov/pub/time.series/oe/
  • API: https://api.bls.gov/publicAPI/v2/timeseries/data/
  • OEWS table reference: https://www.bls.gov/oes/tables.htm

The requested SOC 15-1256 is not present in the current OEWS occupation metadata. Current metadata includes 15-1252 Software Developers and 15-1253 Software Quality Assurance Analysts and Testers, so I used 15-1253 as the documented substitute for the requested QA/testing companion code.

Pearson correlations between OEWS state employment and our state-level SWE posting counts are high:

OEWS measure our measure states r 15-1252 employment all LinkedIn SWE 51 0.981 15-1252 employment scraped 2026 SWE 51 0.975 15-1252 employment arshkon SWE 51 0.973 15-1252 + 15-1253 employment all LinkedIn SWE 51 0.982 15-1252 + 15-1253 employment scraped 2026 SWE 51 0.974 15-1252 + 15-1253 employment arshkon SWE 51 0.974

This clears the target r > 0.80 geographic sanity check. It should not be read as proof that the sample is representative in levels: OEWS is an employment stock, while our data are LinkedIn postings filtered through historical Kaggle snapshots and a 26-metro scrape.

docs.title: Industry Distribution
docs.text:
Our internal LinkedIn industry distribution is available for arshkon and scraped; asaniczka has zero known company_industry rows in the default SWE frame. Top known industries:

source top industries arshkon 2024 IT Services and IT Consulting 25.8%; Software Development 13.1%; Financial Services 8.4%; Staffing and Recruiting 5.8%; Information Services 3.2% scraped 2026 Software Development 26.0%; IT Services and IT Consulting 12.5%; Financial Services 7.2%; Technology, Information and Internet 4.9%; Defense and Space Manufacturing 3.9%

OEWS industry-specific retrieval was not completed. The state-level API calls succeeded, but the later level-4 industry retrieval hit the BLS unkeyed daily request threshold. The alternative current OEWS bulk file is 316 MB, so I did not download it for this memory-disciplined task. Treat industry benchmarking as incomplete.

docs.title: FRED JOLTS Information Sector
docs.text:
FRED CSV succeeded:

https://fred.stlouisfed.org/graph/fredgraph.csv?id=JTU5100JOL,JTU5100HIL,JTU5100LDL

The series are information-sector, not seasonally adjusted levels in thousands:

date openings hires layoffs/discharges 2024-01-01 166 79 22 2024-04-01 98 71 28 2026-02-01 92 84 55

The latest available FRED observation in the downloaded file is February 2026. The scraped window, 2026-03-19 to 2026-04-13, sits just after a period where information-sector openings were below January 2024 and layoffs/discharges were much higher. This is useful macro context, not a SWE-specific labor-demand control.

docs.title: What The Sample Represents
docs.text:
The sample represents English, date-valid LinkedIn SWE postings from two 2024 LinkedIn Kaggle snapshots and the current 2026 scraped LinkedIn window. It is strongest for posting-content and posting-composition comparisons within LinkedIn, especially all-SWE, broad senior, and powered low-YOE junior definitions.

It cannot support claims about total SWE employment, unemployment, wages, all job boards, non-US markets, or complete vacancy flow. It also cannot cleanly separate true market change from source-instrument differences without the required arshkon-vs-asaniczka calibration checks and sensitivity analyses.

docs.title: Surprises & Unexpected Patterns
docs.text:
J2 barely improves power over J1. Combining entry and associate sounds like it should solve thin junior cells, but arshkon moves only from 175 to 190 rows and scraped from 1,275 to 1,327.

The director-only senior definition is directionally interesting but inferentially unusable. T30 found S2 moves up, but T07 shows arshkon has only 13 S2 rows.

The geographic correlation with OEWS is extremely high despite the scraped source being a 26-metro design. This supports geographic face validity, but it may mainly reflect the dominance of large SWE states such as CA, TX, NY, WA, VA, and MA.

Null metro exclusions are larger than expected: 31.4% of 2024 rows and 25.2% of 2026 rows are outside the metro-known frame. This matters for any metro heterogeneity claim.

The FRED context is less benign than a simple "AI-era growth" story: information-sector job openings are lower in the latest available observation than in January 2024, while layoffs/discharges are much higher.

docs.title: Downstream Actions
docs.text:
Use feasibility_summary.csv as the Gate 1 power table. For junior Wave 2/3 analyses, keep T30's J1 as the high-label-quality estimand but do not rely on it alone for modest effects; pair it with J3/J4 and state when a J1 null is power-limited. For senior analyses, use S1 primary and S4 validation; treat S2 and S3 as descriptive/sensitivity only.

Use 25 metros for stricter >=100-per-period metro analysis. Include New York City only in a >=50 threshold specification or a separately flagged sensitivity.

For company-panel work, the 240-company overlap is enough to proceed, but use company weighting/capping or company fixed effects because the panel is heavily skewed toward large 2026 employers.

docs.title: Headline
docs.text:
The strongest distributional facts are not a simple junior decline. In the default LinkedIn SWE frame, pooled-2024 to scraped-2026 junior shares rise under all J1-J4 definitions, but label-based J1/J2 are source-sensitive: arshkon-only to scraped moves slightly down while YOE-based J3/J4 move up. The more stable findings are: scraped 2026 has a large unknown-seniority pool, raw descriptions are much longer, AI/LLM/CI-CD/API/observability indicators rise sharply, and broad senior shares fall while director-only labels rise.

The main anomaly is measurement/composition structure: low-YOE postings are usually not labeled entry. In scraped 2026, 72.4% of yoe_extracted <= 2 rows are seniority_final = 'unknown' and 21.5% are mid-senior; only 5.5% are J1 entry. That is a boundary-labeling problem, not a clean junior-volume story.

docs.title: Methodology
docs.text:
Script: exploration/scripts/T08_distribution_profile.py.

Frame: data/unified.parquet, source_platform = 'linkedin', is_english = true, date_flag = 'ok', is_swe = true. Current default frame has 63,701 LinkedIn SWE rows: arshkon 4,691, asaniczka 18,129, scraped March 2026 19,777, scraped April 2026 21,104.

Memory posture: DuckDB PRAGMA memory_limit='4GB' and PRAGMA threads=1. The script materializes only grouped tables and small plotting frames. It loads shared artifacts rather than rebuilding them: T30 seniority panel, T09 archetype labels, cleaned text, and the shared tech matrix.

Keyword definitions and denominators are documented in exploration/tables/T08/metric_definitions_and_denominators.csv. AI prevalence uses selected shared tech-matrix indicators for AI tools/domains, excluding generic numpy/pandas/scipy/sklearn. Org/scope language uses LLM-cleaned text only. Manual context audit: AI keyword screen 49/50 true positive precision; org/scope screen 50/50, saved in keyword_precision_audit.csv.

Figures are limited to four:

  • exploration/figures/T08/distribution_histograms.png
  • exploration/figures/T08/seniority_panel_changes.png
  • exploration/figures/T08/seniority_and_aggregator_profile.png
  • exploration/figures/T08/domain_archetype_decomposition.png

docs.title: Seniority Panel
docs.text:
Junior definitions, pooled 2024 to scraped 2026:

Definition Meaning Share of all 2024 -> 2026 Share of known 2024 -> 2026 Source-restricted arshkon -> scraped Verdict J1 seniority_final = 'entry' 1.66% -> 3.12% (+1.46 pp) 2.70% -> 6.70% (+3.99 pp) 3.73% -> 3.12% (-0.61 pp all) Pooled up, source-sensitive J2 entry or associate 1.83% -> 3.25% (+1.41 pp) 2.98% -> 6.97% (+3.99 pp) 4.05% -> 3.25% (-0.80 pp all) Same as J1; little added power J3 yoe_extracted <= 2 7.74% -> 11.60% (+3.86 pp) 11.22% -> 17.19% (+5.98 pp) 14.98% -> 17.19% (+2.22 pp known) Up under both source choices J4 yoe_extracted <= 3 17.68% -> 22.77% (+5.08 pp) 25.63% -> 33.74% (+8.12 pp) 31.16% -> 33.74% (+2.59 pp known) Up, broad low-YOE sensitivity

Agreement verdict: J1-J4 agree on pooled direction, but none is cleanly above within-2024 source variation. T30 signal/noise ratios for known-share effects are J1 0.67, J2 0.62, J3 1.30, J4 1.20. Treat this as a measurement puzzle, not a headline junior-rise claim.

Senior definitions:

Definition Meaning Share of all 2024 -> 2026 Share of known 2024 -> 2026 Verdict S1 mid-senior or director 59.57% -> 43.33% (-16.25 pp) 97.02% -> 93.03% (-3.99 pp) Down S2 director 0.46% -> 0.72% (+0.26 pp) 0.75% -> 1.55% (+0.80 pp) Up but sparse/descriptive S3 title-keyword senior 1.95% -> 0.37% in T30 artifact same Down, artifact-prone; T08 raw-title rebuild also down S4 yoe_extracted >= 5 45.04% -> 38.99% (-6.06 pp) 65.28% -> 57.79% (-7.50 pp) Down

Agreement verdict: 3 of 4 senior variants point down; S2 director-only rises and should be interpreted as top-ladder/sparse-label evidence, not contradiction-free senior growth.

docs.title: Main Distribution Findings
docs.text:
1. Description length grew materially beyond within-2024 instrument variation. Mean raw description length rises from 3,763 chars pooled 2024 to 4,912 chars scraped 2026 (+1,149); median rises from 3,594 to 4,862 (+1,269). In mid-senior rows, arshkon-to-scraped growth is 3.62x the arshkon-vs-asaniczka gap.

  2. AI/tool/domain prevalence is a large calibrated change. Selected AI tech-matrix prevalence rises from 3.75% pooled 2024 to 23.70% scraped 2026 (+19.94 pp). In mid-senior rows: arshkon 5.43%, asaniczka 3.55%, scraped 25.77%; calibration ratio 10.86. The audited AI context sample had 98% precision, with one false positive from MCP as a legacy certification.

  3. The low-YOE/entry-label split is the central junior anomaly. Among scraped 2026 J1 entry rows, 75.1% have missing YOE and only 20.4% have yoe_extracted <= 2. Among scraped 2026 low-YOE rows, only 5.5% are labeled entry.

  4. Arshkon native-entry is not ground truth. Arshkon seniority_native = 'entry' has 738 rows; among 458 YOE-known rows, mean YOE is 4.18, median is 3, 42.6% require 5+ years, and only 28.6% require <=2 years. Native entry can be a diagnostic, not a clean baseline.

  5. Tech stack intensity rises. Mean count of true shared tech indicators rises from 5.20 pooled 2024 to 7.26 scraped 2026 (+2.06). Mid-senior tech count calibration ratio is 5.10. The largest stack movers include Python (+17.0 pp pooled-to-scraped), CI/CD (+18.2 pp), API design (+14.4 pp), observability (+12.0 pp), LLM (+12.0 pp), Kubernetes (+9.0 pp), and AWS (+8.7 pp). CI/CD is not cleanly above within-2024 noise in the mid-senior calibration ratio (1.54), so keep it as a candidate rather than a settled temporal claim.

docs.title: Domain Decomposition
docs.text:
T09 archetype coverage is high in historical rows and limited in scraped: arshkon 97.5%, asaniczka 92.6%, scraped March 31.6%, scraped April 29.1%. Domain results are therefore labeled-subset evidence.

In the labeled subset, archetype composition shifts strongly: AI LLM Platforms rise from 3.1% to 17.4%, Product Backend from 6.9% to 15.2%, and Cloud DevOps from 10.0% to 15.9%; Enterprise Application Support falls from 33.9% to 18.3%.

The junior-share decomposition does not support domain recomposition as the main explanation for pooled junior increases:

Definition Total change Within-domain component Between-domain component Interpretation J1 +3.16 pp +3.30 pp -0.15 pp Within-domain rise, not domain mix J2 +3.38 pp +3.61 pp -0.23 pp Same J3 +5.97 pp +6.77 pp -0.80 pp Same, between-domain slightly offsets J4 +8.64 pp +8.40 pp +0.24 pp Mostly within-domain

Subpopulation anomaly: Cloud DevOps Infrastructure is consistently least junior-heavy in scraped 2026 (J1 2.1%, J3 9.3%, J4 24.7%), while Large Employer Data Platforms is unusually low-YOE-heavy (J1 12.0%, J3 37.1%, J4 60.8%). T09 already flagged this archetype as partly employer-template-driven, so do not overinterpret it as a clean domain effect.

docs.title: Sensitivity Checks
docs.text:
Aggregator exclusion: J1/J2/J3/J4 directions are stable. S1/S4 stay down and S2 stays up. AI prevalence and tech count are stable. Aggregator exclusion attenuates broad-senior declines, especially raw-title S3, but does not flip the senior verdict.

Company cap: capping at 50 postings per company leaves J1/J2 growth almost unchanged but materially reduces low-YOE growth: J3 pooled-to-scraped falls from +5.98 pp to +3.04 pp; J4 from +8.12 pp to +4.80 pp. Company concentration is a first-order mechanism for YOE-based junior movement.

SWE tier exclusion: excluding swe_classification_tier = 'title_lookup_llm' preserves the main directions: J1 +1.45 pp all, J3 +6.00 pp known, S1 -16.08 pp all, S4 -7.39 pp known.

Source restriction: label-based J1/J2 are the sensitive case. Pooled 2024 to scraped is up, but arshkon-only to scraped is slightly down. YOE-based J3/J4 are up under both source choices.

Within-2024 calibration: description length, AI prevalence, org/scope language, tech count, Python, Kubernetes, AWS, LLM, observability, and API design are above source noise. Junior/senior share effects are mostly not above source noise except sparse S2 and artifact-prone S3.

Indeed cross-platform: scraped Indeed and scraped LinkedIn broadly agree in 2026. Indeed has longer descriptions (median 5,229-5,391 vs LinkedIn 4,803-4,920), similar raw-regex AI rates (35.6-36.8% vs 35.2-35.7%), similar J1 entry shares (~3.0% vs 2.6-3.6%), and similar J3 low-YOE shares (~17.9-18.4% vs 17.0-17.4%). Seniority unknown remains high on both platforms.

Company size/posting volume: within arshkon, the largest company-size quartile has higher J1 (4.64%) and J3 (19.90%) than the smallest quartile (2.92%, 11.95%). In scraped 2026, the highest posting-volume quartile has lower J1 (2.89%) than low-volume quartiles (4.08-4.37%), but comparable/higher J3 (17.74%) and higher aggregator share (18.38%).

docs.title: Anomaly List
docs.text: Severity Area Finding Gate 2 action High Text coverage Scraped LinkedIn cleaned-text coverage averages 30.7%. Treat text/domain findings as labeled-subset evidence. High Seniority Scraped seniority unknown share averages 53.4%. Keep all/known denominators and YOE validators. High Native labels Arshkon native-entry rows have 42.6% YOE>=5 among YOE-known. Do not treat native entry as ground truth. Medium Description length Length growth exceeds within-2024 variation. Normalize text metrics by posting and length. Medium AI keywords AI prevalence is a large calibrated increase. Validate downstream AI requirement semantics by subpattern. Medium Domain labels T09 coverage in scraped is ~30%. Domain claims need coverage caveats. Medium Metric changes Top changes mix real content and pipeline coverage. Separate market movement from source/coverage artifacts. Low Company size company_size is heavy-tailed in arshkon. Use quartiles/log transforms, not raw means.

docs.title: Ranked Changes
docs.text:
Largest pooled-2024 to scraped-2026 changes in the broad metric screen:

Metric Change Median description length +1,268.5 chars Mean description length +1,148.9 chars Mean tech indicator count +2.06 indicators LLM-cleaned text labeled share -64.5 pp Mean known YOE -0.64 years AI keyword prevalence +19.9 pp Seniority source unknown +29.5 pp Seniority source LLM -18.6 pp CI/CD prevalence +18.2 pp Python prevalence +17.0 pp

Interpretation: the top-ranked list mixes substantive changes with measurement artifacts. LLM coverage and seniority-source movement are pipeline/sample facts; AI, tech count, Python, CI/CD, API design, and observability are substantive screens requiring downstream semantic validation.

docs.title: Surprises & Unexpected Patterns
docs.text:
• The most important junior result is disagreement, not direction: employer entry labels and low-YOE proxies capture mostly different rows.
  • Domain recomposition does not explain the labeled-subset junior increase; within-domain changes dominate J1-J4.
  • AI LLM Platforms is not the least junior-heavy archetype; Cloud DevOps is.
  • Arshkon native entry is much noisier than expected and often not low-YOE.
  • Company capping materially reduces YOE-based junior growth but barely changes label-based J1/J2, implying company mix matters more for YOE floors than explicit entry labels.

docs.title: Data Caveats
docs.text:
• Asaniczka has no native entry labels; any native-label diagnostic is arshkon-only.
  • seniority_final is conservative and unknown-heavy. Dropping unknowns is not representative.
  • T09 archetypes cover only LLM-cleaned rows with sufficient text; scraped 2026 domain evidence is subset evidence.
  • Remote flags remain a 2024 artifact and are not interpreted here.
  • Company industry is missing for asaniczka and should not be pooled as a full historical control.
  • Posting age is nearly unavailable: >99% missing in scraped LinkedIn and 100% missing in historical sources.

docs.title: Downstream Action Items
docs.text:
1. Treat junior-share direction as unresolved/source-sensitive. Use J1 for employer-labeled junior, J3 for powered low-YOE validation, and J4 as broad sensitivity.
  2. Investigate the low-YOE unknown pool directly: title families, companies, domains, and whether descriptions use senior-scope language despite low YOE.
  3. For AI/LLM claims, split AI-tool requirements from AI/ML domain roles. The broad AI screen is strong, but downstream RQ claims need subpattern-level validation.
  4. Company-cap or model company/posting-volume effects in every YOE-based junior analysis.
  5. Use T09 domains only with explicit coverage reporting; consider expanding LLM-cleaned text coverage before making full-corpus domain claims.

docs.title: T09. Posting Archetype Discovery / Methods Laboratory
Headline Finding
docs.text:
Natural SWE posting structure is mostly domain/technology structure, not seniority structure. In the chosen NMF-8 taxonomy, Normalized Mutual Information is highest for tech_domain (0.205), then top-company grouping (0.098), source (0.047), period (0.043), and year (0.041). Seniority alignment is near-zero (0.0069). The market-level archetype shifts are therefore more consistent with domain/source/company recomposition than with postings falling into naturally separate junior/mid/senior language clusters.

The strongest temporal movement in the LLM-cleaned labeled subset is AI/LLM expansion: AI LLM Platforms rises from 3.1% of labeled 2024 rows to 17.4% of labeled 2026 rows. Product Backend Engineering and Cloud DevOps Infrastructure also grow, while Enterprise Application Support, Java Dotnet Web Services, and Defense Clearance Systems shrink as corpus shares.

Downstream labels were written to exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_archetype_labels.parquet for 33,741 eligible text_source='llm' rows with at least 100 cleaned characters.

docs.title: Methodology
docs.text:
Primary sample: 8,000 LinkedIn SWE postings from exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_cleaned_text.parquet, joined to swe_embedding_index.parquet and swe_embeddings.npy. All sampled rows use text_source='llm'; raw fallback rows were excluded from modeling. Rows with fewer than 100 cleaned characters were excluded. Company cap was 50 postings per company_name_canonical before sample allocation.

Sample allocation was year-balanced and source-period controlled:

Source-period Rows kaggle_arshkon, 2024-04 2,400 kaggle_asaniczka, 2024-01 1,600 scraped, 2026-03 2,000 scraped, 2026-04 2,000

This gives 4,000 rows from 2024 and 4,000 from 2026 while preferring arshkon inside the historical allocation. Seniority was stratified within each source-period according to available capped counts. Final sample seniority mix: 308 junior, 29 mid, 4,002 senior, and 3,661 unknown. Mid-level is thin because it is thin in the shared artifact, not because of a modeling choice.

Company concentration in the modeling sample was bounded: the largest companies each contribute at most 50 rows (0.6% of the sample), and the top five contribute 250 rows (3.1%). Aggregators are 956 of 8,000 rows: 10.5% in 2024 and 13.4% in 2026.

All DuckDB joins used PRAGMA memory_limit='4GB'; PRAGMA threads=1;. The run used peak RSS about 1.8GB. No preprocessing stages were rerun and no new LLM calls were made.

docs.title: Method Comparison
docs.text:
BERTopic completed on the same 8,000-row sample using shared embeddings, UMAP, HDBSCAN, and min_topic_size=30. The primary seed produced 3 topics, 0.19% outliers, and rough NPMI coherence 0.122. Top terms were a large general requirements topic plus Android and iOS subtopics. This was not stable: across three seeds, ARI averaged 0.341 with a minimum of 0.019, and one seed produced 26 topics with 41.8% outliers. The min_topic_size=20 and 50 sensitivity runs both returned 3 topics in the primary seed.

NMF was more stable and more useful for downstream stratification. On TF-IDF cleaned text:

Method Variant Topics Noise Rough coherence Stability ARI BERTopic min_topic_size=30 primary seed 3 0.2% 0.122 0.341 mean NMF k=5 5 0.0% 0.169 0.9998 NMF k=8 8 0.0% 0.211 0.9993 NMF k=12 12 0.0% 0.367 0.9961 NMF k=15 15 0.0% 0.397 0.7225

I chose NMF k=8 for the shared label artifact. k=12 had higher coherence but split the taxonomy more finely than downstream Wave 2/3 tasks need; k=15 became materially less stable. BERTopic is useful as a diagnostic because it surfaced mobile subtopics, but it is too seed-sensitive here to serve as the primary archetype layer.

Method alignment was limited. BERTopic's general requirements topic overlaps most with NMF's Enterprise Application Support; its Android/iOS subtopics overlap weakly with broad NMF components. This is a real methods finding: density-based clustering can discover small platform-specific pockets, while NMF gives a more stable market-wide domain taxonomy.

docs.title: Chosen Archetypes
docs.text:
The final NMF-8 archetypes and top terms are:

Archetype Top terms Product Backend Engineering ll, backend, features, help, role, looking, ownership, teams, react, problems, deliver, improve Enterprise Application Support requirements, ability, provide, issues, customer, related, ensure, procedures, including, documentation, application, communication Java Dotnet Web Services java, angular, javascript, net, api, react, apis, sql, rest, microservices, boot, application Cloud DevOps Infrastructure aws, ci/cd, azure, pipelines, terraform, kubernetes, environments, deployment, scripting, docker Defense Clearance Systems clearance, required, active, obtain, requirements, program, citizenship, dod, ts/sci, degree AI LLM Platforms ai, models, pipelines, workflows, ai/ml, llms, llm, frameworks, rag, python, generative, agent Embedded Firmware Systems embedded, firmware, linux, c/c, drivers, real-time, rtos, protocols, embedded linux, debugging Large Employer Data Platforms aws, qualifications, java, python, scala, sql, year, languages, degree, millions americans

Characterization on all labeled rows:

Archetype Labeled rows Avg tech count Avg YOE known J1 entry share J3 YOE<=2 share Product Backend Engineering 3,358 5.24 4.96 4.9% 13.2% Enterprise Application Support 9,508 3.39 5.33 4.8% 14.6% Java Dotnet Web Services 5,917 8.69 5.52 4.9% 11.3% Cloud DevOps Infrastructure 4,107 8.89 5.48 2.9% 9.0% Defense Clearance Systems 3,781 4.56 6.82 3.7% 12.4% AI LLM Platforms 2,824 7.54 4.83 7.9% 15.5% Embedded Firmware Systems 2,567 3.47 5.25 5.5% 14.6% Large Employer Data Platforms 1,679 8.14 4.30 5.9% 20.7%

Entry-share caveat: J1-J4 all generally rise from 2024 to 2026 within archetypes, so these labels do not support a simple "entry disappeared in AI-heavy domains" reading. The strongest low-entry archetype is Cloud DevOps Infrastructure, which has the lowest J1 and J3 shares and almost no J3/J4 growth. AI LLM Platforms is not especially entry-poor in the labeled subset; its J1 share rises from 4.6% to 9.0%, while J3 moves only from 14.1% to 15.9%.

docs.title: Temporal Dynamics
docs.text:
Year-level archetype proportions among labeled rows:

Archetype 2024 share 2026 share Change AI LLM Platforms 3.1% 17.4% +14.3 pp Product Backend Engineering 6.9% 15.2% +8.3 pp Cloud DevOps Infrastructure 10.0% 15.9% +6.0 pp Embedded Firmware Systems 7.5% 7.8% +0.3 pp Large Employer Data Platforms 5.4% 4.2% -1.3 pp Defense Clearance Systems 13.4% 7.4% -6.0 pp Java Dotnet Web Services 19.8% 13.7% -6.1 pp Enterprise Application Support 33.9% 18.3% -15.6 pp

The AI/LLM expansion survives the essential sensitivities. Excluding aggregators gives +14.5 pp; excluding swe_classification_tier='title_lookup_llm' gives +14.5 pp. Large Employer Data Platforms is the fragile case: it falls by -1.3 pp in all rows but rises by +1.9 pp after excluding aggregators, so downstream agents should not treat that small trend as robust.

docs.title: Dominant Structure Test
docs.text:
NMI between NMF archetype labels and candidate explanatory structures:

Factor NMI Tech/domain signal 0.205 Top-30 company group 0.098 Source 0.047 Period 0.043 Year 0.041 SWE classification tier 0.016 Seniority 3-level 0.0069 Aggregator flag 0.0058

Interpretation: the archetypes are not seniority archetypes. They are mostly domain/technology archetypes, with a secondary company/source component. This supports using T09 labels for domain stratification in T11, T15, T16, T20, and T28, but not as a substitute for seniority definitions.

docs.title: Sensitivity Checks
docs.text:
Aggregator exclusion: the main growth/shrinkage story is stable for AI/LLM, product backend, cloud/devops, enterprise support, defense, and Java/.NET services. Aggregator exclusion changes magnitudes and flips only the small Large Employer Data Platforms trend.

Description text source: raw-description NMF on the same 8,000 rows is not a valid substitute for LLM-cleaned text. Clean-vs-raw assignment NMI is only 0.199. Raw top terms are dominated by boilerplate and company/source artifacts: EEO language, Capital One, TikTok, JPMorgan Chase, Google, salary/benefits language, and location strings. This is the strongest validation of the Gate 1.5 instruction to use text_source='llm' for text-dependent modeling.

SWE classification tier: excluding title_lookup_llm preserves the main AI/LLM, product backend, cloud/devops, enterprise-support, defense, and Java/.NET directions. It does not resolve the Large Employer Data Platforms fragility.

docs.title: Coverage And Caveats
docs.text:
Label artifact coverage is limited to LLM-cleaned rows with at least 100 cleaned characters:

Source-period Labels kaggle_asaniczka 2024-01 16,786 kaggle_arshkon 2024-04 4,575 scraped 2026-03 6,243 scraped 2026-04 6,137

Raw fallback rows are intentionally unlabeled: 1,092 asaniczka rows, 4 arshkon rows, 13,452 scraped March rows, and 14,895 scraped April rows. Therefore scraped 2026 archetype distributions describe the LLM-cleaned subset, not the full scraped SWE corpus.

NMF component names are descriptive labels, not hand-validated occupational categories. Large Employer Data Platforms still contains some employer-template residue, visible in terms such as millions americans; downstream uses should keep company capping and raw-text boilerplate risks in view.

NMF convergence warnings appeared at 350 iterations for some fits. Stability remained high for k=8 (mean ARI 0.999), so this is a numerical caveat rather than a failed run.

docs.title: Surprises & Unexpected Patterns
docs.text:
• Seniority is almost absent from the unsupervised structure. This weakens any expectation that junior and senior postings naturally form separate semantic clusters.
  • AI/LLM is the clearest growing archetype, but it is not the least junior-heavy archetype in the LLM-labeled subset.
  • BERTopic mostly found one general topic plus mobile subtopics under the primary seed, then changed substantially under another seed. The small mobile pockets are real enough to notice but not stable enough for the shared taxonomy.
  • Raw descriptions recreate company and compliance boilerplate more than job-content archetypes. This is more severe than a mild noise issue.
  • Defense/clearance is a distinct archetype and shrinks as a labeled-row share, but its within-archetype YOE profile remains senior-heavy.

docs.title: Downstream Use
docs.text:
Use exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_archetype_labels.parquet by joining on uid. The artifact has exactly the requested columns: uid, archetype, archetype_name.

Recommended use: domain stratification for text, complexity, seniority-boundary, company, and geography analyses. Do not use these labels as causal treatment categories, and do not infer full-corpus scraped proportions without reporting LLM-cleaned coverage.

Primary output tables:

  • exploration/tables/T09/methods_comparison.csv
  • exploration/tables/T09/nmf_k8_top_terms.csv
  • exploration/tables/T09/archetype_characterization.csv
  • exploration/tables/T09/archetype_period_distribution.csv
  • exploration/tables/T09/archetype_entry_panel.csv
  • exploration/tables/T09/dominant_structure_nmi.csv
  • exploration/tables/T09/description_text_source_sensitivity.csv
  • exploration/tables/T09/label_coverage_by_source_period.csv

Figures:

  • exploration/figures/T09/embedding_umap_panels.png
  • exploration/figures/T09/embedding_pca_panels.png

docs.title: Headline Finding
docs.text:
The 2026 scraped LinkedIn SWE title space is not simply more senior or more fragmented. The clearest title-taxonomy change is a rise in AI/ML/data/agent hybrid labels: 23.7% of scraped 2026 SWE postings have one of those raw-title terms, versus 12.7% in arshkon 2024 and 9.6% in pooled 2024. This survives aggregator exclusion almost unchanged: 23.3% scraped 2026 versus 13.0% arshkon and 9.5% pooled 2024.

Title fragmentation is mixed. Scraped 2026 has fewer unique raw-title keys per 1,000 postings than arshkon alone (480.5 vs 508.4), but more than pooled 2024 (480.5 vs 444.6). After excluding aggregators the same pattern remains (509.3 scraped, 524.3 arshkon, 462.4 pooled 2024). The safest interpretation is source-composition plus mild 2026 fragmentation, not clean standardization.

docs.title: Methodology
docs.text:
Sample: default LinkedIn SWE frame, source_platform='linkedin', English, date_flag='ok', is_swe=true. Row counts: 4,691 arshkon, 18,129 asaniczka, and 40,881 scraped LinkedIn SWE rows across 2026-03 and 2026-04.

Titles were normalized from the raw title column with a seniority-preserving key: lowercase, whitespace/punctuation cleanup, common Sr./Jr. expansion, and removal only of remote/hybrid/contract suffixes. I did not use title_normalized, because it strips seniority markers.

Content similarity uses only shared LLM-cleaned text (text_source='llm') and company-token stopwords. For the 10 most common titles with at least 20 LLM-text rows in both arshkon and scraped, I sampled at most 300 rows per period/title and computed TF-IDF centroid cosine.

Primary outputs:

  • exploration/tables/T10/title_concentration.csv
  • exploration/tables/T10/new_2026_titles_vs_arshkon_top200.csv
  • exploration/tables/T10/new_2026_titles_tagged.csv
  • exploration/tables/T10/raw_title_seniority_marker_prevalence.csv
  • exploration/tables/T10/hybrid_ai_ml_data_title_prevalence.csv
  • exploration/tables/T10/same_title_tfidf_similarity.csv
  • exploration/tables/T10/shared_title_seniority_panel.csv

Figures:

  • exploration/figures/T10/title_concentration.png
  • exploration/figures/T10/hybrid_title_share.png
  • exploration/figures/T10/raw_title_marker_share.png
  • exploration/figures/T10/same_title_tfidf_similarity.png

docs.title: Hybrid AI/Data Titles
docs.text:
AI/ML/data/LLM/agent title prevalence:

Spec arshkon pooled 2024 scraped 2026 All rows 12.7% 9.6% 23.7% Exclude aggregators 13.0% 9.5% 23.3%

The increase is driven mainly by AI/artificial intelligence title terms: 1.2% in pooled 2024 to 10.5% in scraped 2026. Agent terms rise from 0.03% to 1.08%; LLM terms remain small but increase from 0.07% to 0.34%.

docs.title: New And Disappeared Titles
docs.text:
Among the top 200 2026 titles absent from arshkon, 102 title keys are also absent from pooled 2024. Those true-new-vs-pooled titles account for 1,094 scraped postings. The largest category is AI/ML/Data: 38 title keys and 402 scraped postings. Examples include Senior AI/ML Engineer, Applied AI Engineer, AI Platform Engineer, AI Agent Engineer, and AI Automation Engineer.

The arshkon-only disappeared list is mostly legacy/source-specific rather than a clean labor-market disappearance signal. Examples such as Drupal Developer, DevOps Architect, Sr. Java Developer, Senior Database Developer, and PHP Developer often appear in asaniczka, so they are absent from scraped but not necessarily absent from the 2024 market.

docs.title: Same-Title Content Similarity
docs.text:
Same-title TF-IDF cosine is highest for generic Software Engineer and Senior Software Engineer (about 0.79), and lower for more domain-specific titles:

Title 2024/2026 cosine Embedded Software Engineer 0.595 Java Developer 0.598 Site Reliability Engineer 0.618 Cloud Engineer 0.661 Full Stack Engineer 0.673 Data Engineer 0.783 Software Engineer 0.791

This suggests some same-title semantic drift, especially in embedded, Java, SRE, and cloud roles, while generic software-engineer titles remain semantically broad in both periods.

docs.title: Title Seniority Markers
docs.text:
Using arshkon as the 2024 comparator, raw-title staff markers more than double: 2.5% to 5.8%. Senior rises modestly from 27.1% to 29.0%, principal from 2.8% to 3.7%, and junior/entry from 2.0% to 2.5%. Lead is flat and architect declines from 1.7% to 0.4%.

This is not robust as a generic senior-title inflation claim because asaniczka 2024 is much more senior-title-heavy than arshkon. For example, senior is 45.5% in asaniczka, so pooled 2024 exceeds scraped 2026. The robust title-marker finding is narrower: staff rises even against both historical sources.

docs.title: Surprises And Unexpected Patterns
docs.text:
• The top "new" title by count is Software Engineer [Multiple Positions Available], which is likely a template or posting-format artifact rather than a new occupation.
  • Many titles absent from arshkon are present in asaniczka, so arshkon-only "new" and "disappeared" lists are source-sensitive.
  • Same-title seniority shifts are not one-directional. Software Engineer becomes more senior-labeled among known-seniority rows, but other common titles split by domain and YOE definition.
  • Title fragmentation is lower than arshkon but higher than pooled 2024, showing how much the conclusion depends on the historical baseline.

docs.title: Sensitivity Checks
docs.text:
Aggregator exclusion does not explain the AI/ML/data title increase. Hybrid title prevalence changes from 23.7% to 23.3% in scraped 2026 and from 9.6% to 9.5% in pooled 2024.

Seniority-marker title claims are source-sensitive. Staff-title growth is robust; broad senior-title growth is not robust to using pooled 2024 rather than arshkon alone.

Same-title content similarity is restricted to LLM-cleaned text. This avoids boilerplate but limits scraped coverage to the Stage 9 labeled subset.

docs.title: Caveats
docs.text:
Title strings are employer-facing labels, not occupational truth. A title absent from arshkon can be a source-instrument artifact, a spelling/formatting difference, or a real emerging role. Treat new_vs_pooled_2024=true as the stricter list.

The title-seniority panel has thin denominators for some high-frequency title keys after restricting to known seniority or known YOE. Use it diagnostically, not as a headline estimator.

docs.title: Downstream Action Items
docs.text:
1. Gate 2 should verify the hybrid AI/ML/data title prevalence and the true-new-vs-pooled emerging-title counts.
  2. T14/T15 should check whether AI title growth corresponds to AI tech-stack growth or mostly title signaling.
  3. T16 should test whether AI/ML/Data emerging titles are concentrated in new companies or common companies.
  4. Use staff title growth as the title-inflation lead, not broad senior, which is source-sensitive.

docs.title: T11. Requirements Complexity And Credential Stacking
Headline Finding
docs.text:
Requirements became materially broader in the LLM-cleaned SWE subset. Mean requirement_breadth rises from 7.23 in pooled 2024 to 9.39 in scraped 2026 (+2.16 distinct requirement signals). Mean credential_stack_depth rises from 2.65 to 3.13 (+0.49 of 7 categories), and the share of postings with stack depth at least 4 rises from about 24% in each 2024 source to 38.9% in scraped 2026.

This is not just an aggregate seniority-composition result. Requirement breadth rises under every junior panel definition and every senior panel definition. For junior definitions: J1 +1.74, J2 +1.84, J3 +1.75, J4 +2.08. For senior definitions: S1 +2.40, S2 +6.16, S3 +2.32, S4 +2.61.

The strongest components are technology breadth, AI-specific requirements, and organizational-scope language. Tech density per 1,000 characters barely changes in all-SWE (+0.11 and below within-2024 calibration), so technology count growth should be read as broader stacks in longer/richer descriptions, not necessarily higher density.

docs.title: Methodology
docs.text:
Primary sample: shared swe_cleaned_text.parquet rows with text_source='llm', joined to swe_tech_matrix.parquet for technology counts. This gives 34,258 LLM-cleaned LinkedIn SWE rows: 4,687 arshkon, 17,037 asaniczka, and 12,534 scraped rows. Scraped LLM-cleaned coverage remains limited: 6,325 rows in 2026-03 and 6,209 rows in 2026-04, versus 28,347 scraped raw-fallback rows excluded from the primary text-sensitive results.

Metrics:

  • tech_count: distinct shared tech-matrix indicators.
  • ai_requirement_count: AI/domain/tool subset of the tech matrix, excluding generic numpy, pandas, scipy, and ambiguous mcp.
  • requirement_breadth: non-AI tech count + AI count + soft-skill count + org-scope count + strong-management count + education-any + YOE-any.
  • credential_stack_depth: number of non-empty categories among tech, education, YOE, soft skills, org scope, strong management, and AI.
  • tech_density: tech_count per 1,000 cleaned-text characters.
  • scope_density: org-scope count per 1,000 cleaned-text characters.

T30 definitions are reported for all seniority-stratified claims: J1-J4 for junior and S1-S4 for senior. Row flags follow the published T30 definitions; the aggregate T30 panel CSV is copied into the task outputs for traceability.

Custom regex families were semantically checked on 50 sampled surrounding-context matches per major family. Manual precision after tightening:

Family Precision Note Soft skill 94% Requirement-style communication/collaboration/problem-solving/teamwork. Org scope 92% Ownership, end-to-end, cross-functional, stakeholder, autonomy. Management strong 92% Bare hire was removed after a contract-to-hire false positive. Management broad extra 86% Broad coordination context, not people-management evidence. AI requirement 96% Generic scientific libraries and ambiguous mcp excluded.

Primary outputs:

  • exploration/tables/T11/posting_complexity_features.parquet
  • exploration/tables/T11/complexity_effects_by_panel_primary_llm_text.csv
  • exploration/tables/T11/complexity_effects_no_aggregators.csv
  • exploration/tables/T11/complexity_effects_company_cap50.csv
  • exploration/tables/T11/complexity_effects_exclude_entry_specialists.csv
  • exploration/tables/T11/management_term_breakdown.csv
  • exploration/tables/T11/domain_stratified_scope_inflation.csv
  • exploration/tables/T11/top_complexity_outliers_top200.csv

Figures:

  • exploration/figures/T11/junior_complexity_metric_changes.png
  • exploration/figures/T11/credential_stack_depth_means.png
  • exploration/figures/T11/management_trigger_shares.png
  • exploration/figures/T11/domain_stratified_j3_scope_density_change.png

docs.title: Complexity And Stacking
docs.text:
All-SWE LLM-cleaned means:

Metric pooled 2024 scraped 2026 Change Within-2024 diff Requirement breadth 7.23 9.39 +2.16 -0.16 Credential stack depth 2.65 3.13 +0.49 +0.04 Tech count 5.18 6.94 +1.75 -0.16 AI requirement count 0.08 0.53 +0.45 -0.03 Org-scope count 0.57 0.86 +0.30 -0.01 Scope density 0.58 0.80 +0.22 -0.04 Tech density 7.49 7.59 +0.11 -0.22

Requirement breadth and stack depth are clearly above within-2024 instrument variation. Tech density is not; scope density is.

docs.title: Entry-Level Complexity
docs.text:
Every junior definition shows higher requirement breadth in scraped 2026:

Definition pooled 2024 scraped 2026 Change J1: seniority_final='entry' 6.26 8.00 +1.74 J2: entry/associate 6.27 8.12 +1.84 J3: YOE <= 2 7.46 9.21 +1.75 J4: YOE <= 3 7.44 9.51 +2.08

The strongest junior components are tech count and AI count. J1 AI requirement count rises from 0.08 to 0.60; J3 rises from 0.09 to 0.50.

Scope inflation is more definition-sensitive. J1/J2 org-scope counts rise, but J1/J2 scope density is weak relative to within-2024 variation. J3/J4 scope density rises more robustly: J3 +0.17 and J4 +0.19 per 1,000 characters.

docs.title: Senior Complexity And Management
docs.text:
Senior complexity rises under all senior definitions:

Definition Requirement breadth change Stack-depth change Strong-management change S1: mid-senior/director +2.40 +0.59 +0.075 S2: director +6.16 +0.51 +0.260 S3: raw senior title regex +2.32 +0.62 +0.080 S4: YOE >= 5 +2.61 +0.50 +0.062

Management language does not support a simple "senior roles moved away from management" claim in this metric. Strong management rises for S1/S3/S4 and rises sharply for the sparse director-only S2 group. At the all-SWE level, strong management only rises modestly (+0.025) and is close to within-2024 noise, so the senior-specific result is the more relevant one.

The top strong-management triggers are still mentorship-heavy, not direct-report-heavy. In pooled 2024, mentor appears in 10.3% of LLM-text postings and coach in 2.2%; in scraped 2026, mentor is 12.7% and coach is 2.2%. Direct reports, performance reviews, hiring, and headcount are all rare.

The broad tier rises more visibly, but it should be interpreted as coordination/leadership context: team rises from 43.9% to 59.9%, and stakeholder from 15.8% to 23.0%.

docs.title: Domain-Stratified Scope
docs.text:
T09 archetypes show that complexity growth is not only an AI-domain story. Among all LLM-labeled rows, requirement breadth rises in 7 of 8 archetypes. Large increases appear in Java Dotnet Web Services (+2.92), AI LLM Platforms (+2.11), Cloud DevOps Infrastructure (+2.06), and Defense Clearance Systems (+1.94).

For J3 low-YOE rows, requirement breadth also rises in 7 of 8 archetypes. Large Employer Data Platforms is the exception on breadth (-1.52), but its scope density rises (+0.48), suggesting a changing template/domain mix rather than simple deflation.

docs.title: Outliers
docs.text: The top 1% threshold is requirement_breadth >= 23 in the LLM-cleaned sample. Outliers are not pure template bloat. The heuristic split found mostly likely_real_complex_role and mixed_needs_review, with no large standalone template-bloat class. Scraped outliers are still company/aggregator-concentrated: the likely-real subset is 21.6% aggregators and includes Jobs via Dice, JPMorgan Chase & Co., GEICO, Rivago Infotech Inc, and Equifax among top companies.

docs.title: Surprises And Unexpected Patterns
docs.text:
• AI requirements rose inside junior definitions, not only in senior roles.
  • J1 label-based entry postings show breadth growth but weak scope-density growth; low-YOE J3/J4 show stronger scope-density evidence.
  • Senior management language rises when measured with strong patterns, but the rise is mostly mentorship/coordination rather than direct-report management.
  • Technology count rises strongly, while tech density does not. This prevents a clean claim that postings became more tech-dense independent of description length.
  • Complexity growth appears across most T09 domains, so domain recomposition alone is unlikely to explain the full change.

docs.title: Sensitivity Checks
docs.text:
Aggregator exclusion preserves the main result. Requirement breadth changes: all-SWE +2.29, J1 +1.63, J3 +1.82, S1 +2.56, S4 +2.59.

Company cap 50 preserves and slightly strengthens the main result. Requirement breadth changes: all-SWE +2.30, J1 +1.74, J3 +2.07, S1 +2.58, S4 +2.68.

Excluding entry-specialist employers preserves the main result. Requirement breadth changes: all-SWE +2.30, J1 +2.29, J3 +2.13, S1 +2.55, S4 +2.74.

Text-source sensitivity does not flip direction. Including raw fallback increases the magnitude: all-SWE requirement breadth change is +2.64 versus +2.16 in LLM-cleaned text. This likely reflects boilerplate/template contamination in raw scraped descriptions, so the LLM-cleaned estimate should remain primary.

Within-2024 calibration supports requirement breadth, stack depth, AI count, org-scope count, and scope density. It does not support strong claims about tech density, education level, or all-SWE strong-management change.

docs.title: Caveats
docs.text:
The primary text-dependent findings describe the LLM-cleaned subset, not the full scraped corpus. Scraped cleaned-text coverage is about 31% of scraped LinkedIn SWE rows.

Regex features are interpretable indicators, not parsed requirements. The semantic validation removes obvious low-precision patterns, but it cannot distinguish all aspirational requirements from screened requirements.

management_broad_count is intentionally broad. Use it for coordination language, not people-management claims.

S2 director-only cells are small. Treat S2 as top-ladder descriptive evidence, not a powered senior estimate.

docs.title: Downstream Action Items
docs.text:
1. Gate 2 should independently verify all-SWE, J1, J3, S1, and S4 requirement-breadth changes.
  2. T20/T21 should follow up on the senior management result, separating mentorship/orchestration from direct-report management.
  3. T28 should decompose complexity growth into within-domain and between-domain components using T09 archetypes.
  4. Qualitative interviews should test whether junior AI/tech breadth reflects real screening, template inflation, or anticipatory signaling.

docs.title: Headline
docs.text:
The strongest 2026-heavy text movement is a mix of workflow/platform language, exposure-style credentials, systems-scope language, and AI-tool terms. This is not explained away by aggregator rows, company concentration, raw boilerplate, or source restriction. In the primary LLM-cleaned, non-aggregator, company-capped arshkon-vs-scraped comparison, top 2026-heavy terms include workflows, pipelines, familiarity, hands-on, tooling, observability, models, ownership, ai/ml, agent, llm, and rag.

The T13 section-filtered comparison preserves the same broad signal: environments, familiarity, workflows, pipelines, hands-on, tooling, observability, ai/ml, llm, ci/cd, apis, and distributed remain 2026-heavy after restricting to responsibilities/requirements/preferred text.

docs.title: Methodology
docs.text:
Script: exploration/scripts/T12_open_text_evolution.py.

Primary comparison: arshkon 2024 vs scraped 2026, LinkedIn SWE, text_source='llm', aggregators excluded, company cap 50 per corpus/company, company-name stoplist applied, location tokens stripped, unigram log-odds with informative Dirichlet prior.

Primary n: 4,221 arshkon postings from 2,266 companies and 9,531 scraped postings from 3,601 companies. Section-filtered n: 2,496 arshkon and 6,737 scraped. Bigram n: 4,220 and 9,530. Counts for all comparisons are in exploration/tables/T12/corpus_counts_all_comparisons.csv.

T13 dependency: section-filtered text uses requirements_responsibilities_text from exploration/tables/T13/section_text_by_uid.parquet, including responsibilities, requirements, and preferred sections.

BERTopic deviation: not rerun. T09 found BERTopic unstable, and this run followed the OOM guidance by cross-validating against T09 NMF archetype labels instead.

Main outputs:

  • exploration/tables/T12/log_odds_full_top_terms.csv
  • exploration/tables/T12/log_odds_section_filtered_top_terms.csv
  • exploration/tables/T12/log_odds_bigram_top_terms.csv

  • exploration/tables/T12/sensitivity_summary.csv

Figures:

  • exploration/figures/T12/category_summary_full_vs_section.png

docs.title: Main Findings
docs.text:
1. 2026-heavy terms center on workflow/platform scope. Document prevalence rises sharply for workflows (5.2% to 28.1%), pipelines (14.6% to 36.4%), tooling (3.2% to 14.2%), observability (2.6% to 13.8%), ownership (5.2% to 15.9%), end-to-end (5.9% to 16.4%), and distributed (12.0% to 24.8%).

  2. Exposure-style credential language grows. familiarity rises from 12.8% to 38.9%, hands-on from 12.6% to 34.1%, demonstrated from 6.0% to 18.0%, proficiency from 14.3% to 32.6%, and exposure from 4.0% to 12.4%. This looks like screening language broadening, but these are token-level findings and need semantic validation before being converted into requirement prevalence claims.

  3. AI-tool emergence is visible in open terms, not only predefined dictionaries. Emerging terms include ai-assisted (0% to 3.7%), claude (0.02% to 3.7%), cursor (0% to 2.3%), mcp (0.05% to 1.9%, flagged ambiguous), multi-agent (0.09% to 1.8%), langgraph (0% to 1.6%), embeddings (0.09% to 1.4%), ai-enabled (0.05% to 1.3%), llm-based (0.02% to 1.3%), and llm-powered (0.02% to 1.1%).

  4. Bigram results sharpen the interpretation. 2026-heavy phrases include ci/cd pipelines (5.3% to 17.0%), backend apis (0.6% to 3.2%), large models (0.7% to 3.1%), cross-functional collaboration (0.2% to 2.2%), familiarity ci/cd (0.3% to 2.1%), and retrieval-augmented rag (0.09% to 1.6%).

  5. 2024-heavy terms are legacy enterprise/web stack and source artifacts. Arshkon-heavy terms include .net, sql, database, javascript, php, java, azure, mvc, html, jquery, and active directory. These should be read as relative corpus distinctions, not disappearance from the market.

  6. Entry and senior panels move in similar directions. J1-J4 and S1/S3/S4 all show 2026-heavy workflow/credential/AI terms. S2 director-only is flagged n < 100 on the 2024 side and should be treated as descriptive only. The relabeling diagnostic does not look like 2026 entry is simply 2024 senior text: entry-2026-heavy terms include internship, pursuing, gain, and real-world alongside workflows/models/CI-CD.

  7. T09 archetypes cross-validate the open-text signal. In the same primary capped/non-aggregator subset, T09 AI LLM Platforms rises from 4.6% to 17.8% (+13.2 pp), and Product Backend Engineering rises from 8.6% to 18.0% (+9.5 pp). Java Dotnet Web Services falls from 27.0% to 11.9%, matching the 2024-heavy .NET/JavaScript/SQL term pattern.

docs.title: Sensitivity Checks
docs.text:
Aggregator inclusion: top 2026-heavy term overlap with the primary is 0.98.

No company cap: overlap is 0.94.

Raw-description text source: overlap is 0.84. Raw adds boilerplate terms, but the main AI/workflow/platform terms remain.

Source restriction: pooled-2024 vs scraped overlap is 0.82. The signal survives adding asaniczka, though magnitudes shift.

Within-2024 calibration: arshkon-vs-asaniczka top-term overlap with the primary 2026-heavy list is only 0.12. Within-2024 differences are mostly clearance/embedded/program/source-composition terms, not the AI/workflow cluster.

SWE tier: excluding title_lookup_llm gives 0.96 top-term overlap.

docs.title: Surprises & Unexpected Patterns
docs.text:
• The most distinctive 2026 non-AI language is not "more senior" language narrowly; it is workflows, tooling, pipelines, environments, exposure, and ownership.
  • AI-tool emergence includes specific agent-era tools and patterns (Cursor, Claude, MCP, LangGraph, multi-agent) rather than only generic AI.
  • Section filtering does not remove the 2026-heavy signal, so it is not only benefits/about/legal boilerplate.
  • The disappearing-term list is mostly concatenation artifacts such as skillsability and experienceexperience; do not treat it as substantive disappearance.

docs.title: Caveats
docs.text:
• Open-ended token comparisons are discovery tools. Reported term prevalence is token presence after stopword/company stripping, not validated semantic requirement prevalence.
  • Residual noise remains high in top-100 category summaries, especially for generic terms and LLM-cleaned artifacts. Top term context samples are saved in top_term_context_samples.csv; no precision claim is made from the categorizer alone.
  • mcp is flagged as ambiguous because prior validation found non-AI false positives.
  • Scraped cleaned-text coverage is limited: the primary text-dependent findings describe the LLM-cleaned scraped subset, not all scraped LinkedIn postings.
  • Company stopword stripping may remove some real technology/company-overlap tokens; this is the intended tradeoff to avoid company-name domination.

docs.title: Downstream Action Items
docs.text:
1. Build validated requirement families from the open-term candidates before using them as prevalence measures: AI tools, workflow/pipeline ownership, observability, exposure-style credentials, and systems-scope language.
  2. In Gate 2, treat "scope inflation" as plausible but not identical to longer descriptions: T12 terms point to workflow/platform scope, while T13 shows core-section share is roughly flat.
  3. Follow up by company/domain: T09 suggests AI LLM Platforms and Product Backend growth, while Java/.NET and Enterprise Application Support shrink in the LLM-labeled subset.
  4. For paper-grade claims, manually validate stratified contexts for the specific terms that become metrics.

docs.title: Headline
docs.text:
In LLM-cleaned text, 2026 SWE postings are longer and harder to read, but the growth is not mostly benefits/legal/about-company boilerplate. Pooled-2024 to scraped-2026 mean cleaned length rises from 946 to 1,094 characters (+148). The growth decomposes into role-summary (+45.5 chars), responsibilities (+27.0), requirements (+15.1), and still-unclassified cleaned content (+47.3); benefits/about/legal contribute only about +1.0 char in the cleaned-text primary.

The raw-description sensitivity tells a different story: raw scraped descriptions are about 5,000 chars on the same LLM-covered rows and roughly 27% of raw characters fall in benefits/about/legal sections. This is why description_core_llm remains the right primary text source for content claims.

docs.title: Methodology
docs.text:
Script: exploration/scripts/T13_linguistic_structural_evolution.py.

Primary frame: shared LinkedIn SWE text artifact, text_source='llm', nonempty cleaned text. Primary section/tone rows: 4,653 arshkon 2024, 16,992 asaniczka 2024, and 12,456 scraped 2026. Coverage including raw fallback is reported in exploration/tables/T13/text_source_coverage.csv.

Section classifier: regex heading parser for role summary, responsibilities, requirements, preferred, benefits, about company, legal/EEO, and unclassified. Because LLM-cleaned text often removes punctuation but leaves flattened heading tokens, the classifier includes a conservative flattened-heading fallback with plausibility cues. T12 uses requirements_responsibilities_text, which includes responsibilities, requirements, and preferred sections.

Readability: bounded deterministic sample, at most 500 rows per source-period-seniority cell. The sample has 2,402 pooled-2024 rows and 2,652 scraped-2026 rows. Absolute readability scores are inflated because LLM-cleaned text often lacks sentence punctuation; use them as relative metrics only.

Main outputs:

  • exploration/tables/T13/section_text_by_uid.parquet
  • exploration/tables/T13/section_composition_by_corpus.csv
  • exploration/tables/T13/length_growth_section_decomposition.csv
  • exploration/tables/T13/tone_metrics_by_corpus.csv
  • exploration/tables/T13/readability_by_corpus.csv
  • exploration/tables/T13/entry_vs_senior_structure_tone_panel.csv

Figures:

  • exploration/figures/T13/section_composition_stacked.png
  • exploration/figures/T13/readability_metrics.png
  • exploration/figures/T13/tone_marker_density.png
  • exploration/figures/T13/entry_panel_core_section_share.png

docs.title: Main Findings
docs.text:
1. Cleaned-text length growth is real but modest relative to raw growth. Mean LLM-cleaned length rises from 946 chars in pooled 2024 to 1,094 in scraped 2026 (+15.7%). Arshkon-only to scraped is larger (+192 chars), with requirements and role-summary each accounting for about 42% of that arshkon-baseline growth.

  2. Requirements/responsibilities did not expand as a share of cleaned text. Core req/resp/preferred share is nearly flat: pooled 2024 42.0% to scraped 2026 41.5%. In levels, requirements/responsibilities grow, but the whole cleaned posting grows too.

  3. Raw boilerplate is large, cleaned boilerplate is not. On the same LLM-covered rows, raw scraped descriptions average 4,999 chars. Raw scraped section shares are about 18.3% responsibilities, 21.0% requirements, 7.3% preferred, and 26.7% benefits/about/legal. In cleaned scraped text, benefits/about/legal are only about 0.13% of characters.

  4. Readability worsens directionally. In the bounded sample, FK grade rises from 38.7 to 45.9, average sentence length from 56.9 to 78.1 words, and type-token ratio falls from 0.848 to 0.829. Senior rows remain the hardest to read, but all seniority groups move in the same direction.

  5. Tone becomes less direct but more marketed. Pooled imperative markers fall from 0.024 to 0.011 per 1K cleaned chars, and inclusive/direct language also falls. Marketing markers rise from 0.252 to 0.319 per 1K chars; share with any marketing marker rises from 18.9% to 26.4%. Aggregator exclusion does not flip this.

  6. Entry structure changes are not boilerplate-led. Under J1-J4, junior core-section share rises or stays high: J1 37.6% to 44.3%, J2 38.3% to 43.9%, J3 45.4% to 47.8%, J4 45.3% to 46.5%. The S1 senior comparator falls from 43.5% to 41.3%.

docs.title: Surprises & Unexpected Patterns
docs.text:
• LLM-cleaned text often preserves section words but not heading punctuation. A line-heading-only parser would have classified most cleaned text as unsectioned.
  • The strongest raw-text expansion is not a safe content signal; raw descriptions preserve benefits/legal/company sections that the cleaned-text column removes.
  • Direct-address/imperative language declines while marketing language rises, so 2026 postings are not simply more conversational.
  • J1 entry-labeled postings become more core-section-heavy relative to S1 senior postings, which complicates a pure "entry postings are inflated by boilerplate" interpretation.

docs.title: Sensitivity Checks
docs.text:
Aggregator exclusion: tone directions hold. Marketing density is 0.232 to 0.328 per 1K chars with aggregators excluded, versus 0.252 to 0.319 in all rows.

Description text source: raw same-row descriptions produce much higher length and boilerplate shares. This is a material text-source sensitivity and supports using LLM-cleaned text as the primary for T12.

Source restriction: pooled-2024 dampens requirement-growth relative to arshkon-only. Arshkon-only to scraped shows requirements +81.5 chars and role summary +82.4; pooled-2024 to scraped shows requirements +15.1 and role summary +45.5.

docs.title: Caveats
docs.text:
• Section labels are heuristic parser outputs, not hand-validated gold labels.
  • Readability scores are relative only because cleaned text strips or normalizes punctuation.
  • preferred is included in the reusable T12 core text because it is requirement-like, but reports distinguish it from required qualifications.
  • Tone marker context samples are saved in tone_marker_context_samples.csv; no high-precision semantic claim is made from those regexes alone.

docs.title: Downstream Action Items
docs.text:
1. Use section_text_by_uid.parquet for section-filtered text comparisons, but report section coverage and the classifier caveat.
  2. Treat raw-description text as a boilerplate sensitivity, not the primary basis for density/content claims.
  3. In Gate 2, separate length growth from requirement-share growth: cleaned text got longer, but core-section share did not rise in the all-SWE pooled comparison.
  4. For paper-grade claims, manually validate the section classifier on stratified examples from both Kaggle and scraped sources.

docs.title: Headline
docs.text:
The 2026 SWE technology ecosystem is broader and more integrated, not just decorated with a few AI terms. In the default LinkedIn SWE frame from the shared artifacts, 29 of 148 technology indicators are calibrated risers and 4 are calibrated decliners under the primary all-row specification. The biggest calibrated movers are CI/CD, Python, API design, observability, LLM, Kubernetes, AWS, generative AI, Docker, Terraform, and RAG.

The co-occurrence network also changed shape. In the company-capped network, Python moves from a general cloud/devops neighborhood in 2024 into a 2026 AI/LLM cluster with LLM, generative AI, RAG, PyTorch, TensorFlow, Claude, and MLOps. Cloud/devops remains a large, stable cluster, while frontend/API and data/service stacks split into more distinct bundles.

docs.title: Methodology
docs.text:
Script: exploration/scripts/T14_technology_ecosystem.py.

Inputs: exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_tech_matrix.parquet, tech_taxonomy.csv, swe_cleaned_text.parquet, asaniczka_structured_skills.parquet, and narrow metadata columns from data/unified.parquet. The script did not rebuild the technology matrix, recompute embeddings, rerun preprocessing, or make LLM calls.

Frame: default LinkedIn SWE rows already encoded in the shared artifacts: source_platform = 'linkedin', is_english = true, date_flag = 'ok', is_swe = true. The shared matrix has 63,701 postings and 148 binary technology/tool/practice indicators. Indicators are binary mention flags scanned over description_cleaned, which is LLM-cleaned text where available and raw fallback otherwise.

Network specification: company-capped at 50 postings per company_name_canonical x year, technologies included if they appear in at least 1% of postings in either period, edges included if phi coefficient > 0.15 and the co-mention appears across at least 20 distinct companies. Communities use NetworkX greedy modularity.

Key outputs:

  • exploration/tables/T14/technology_shift_classification.csv
  • exploration/tables/T14/cooccurrence_network_edges.csv
  • exploration/tables/T14/community_summary.csv
  • exploration/tables/T14/tech_breadth_t30_panel.csv
  • exploration/tables/T14/ai_integration_length_normalization.csv
  • exploration/tables/T14/structured_vs_extracted_validation.csv
  • exploration/tables/T14/structured_skill_seniority_associations.csv

Figures:

  • exploration/figures/T14/tech_shift_heatmap.png
  • exploration/figures/T14/cooccurrence_networks.png
  • exploration/figures/T14/tech_breadth_seniority_panel.png
  • exploration/figures/T14/ai_integration_stack.png

docs.title: Broad Technology Growth
docs.text:
Primary binary mention rates, pooled 2024 to scraped 2026:

Technology 2024 rate 2026 rate Change Calibration verdict CI/CD 15.4% 33.6% +18.2 pp calibrated rising Python 32.3% 49.4% +17.0 pp calibrated rising API design 13.0% 27.4% +14.4 pp calibrated rising Observability 1.9% 13.9% +12.0 pp calibrated rising LLM 1.0% 13.0% +12.0 pp calibrated rising Kubernetes 13.0% 22.0% +9.0 pp calibrated rising AWS 25.8% 34.5% +8.7 pp calibrated rising Generative AI 0.9% 7.5% +6.7 pp calibrated rising RAG 0.1% 5.2% +5.1 pp calibrated rising

Some apparent risers are real directional changes but not cleanly above within-2024 source noise. TypeScript rises 13.3% to 18.4% (+5.1 pp), and Azure rises 19.4% to 23.7% (+4.2 pp), but both are classified as rising_not_above_2024_noise.

Category-level rates also show ecosystem expansion:

Category 2024 2026 AI tool 2.9% 19.2% AI/ML domain 2.4% 10.8% DevOps 17.9% 32.5% Practice 21.4% 40.6% Cloud 37.0% 46.5% Framework 31.4% 37.3% Testing 5.1% 5.0%

Testing is the notable non-mover. The 2026 expansion is mostly AI, cloud/devops, API/platform, and operational practice language.

docs.title: Network Structure
docs.text:
The company-capped co-occurrence network grows from 239 edges in 2024 to 283 edges in 2026 under identical thresholds.

2024 communities:

  • Cloud/devops/general tooling: Python, AWS, Azure, CI/CD, Git, Kubernetes, Docker, Microservices.
  • Web/API/.NET: SQL, JavaScript, React, API design, C#, TypeScript, HTML, .NET.
  • JVM/data: Java, PostgreSQL, Kafka, MySQL, Scala, Hadoop, Redis, Airflow.
  • Systems/scripting: Linux, C++, Unix, Ruby, MATLAB, Perl, Django, Rust.
  • Small AI/ML cluster: TensorFlow, PyTorch, LLM, fine-tuning, generative AI, Pandas, NumPy, MLOps.

2026 communities:

  • Cloud/devops/platform: AWS, CI/CD, Azure, Kubernetes, Docker, Microservices, Observability, Terraform.
  • AI/LLM/Python: Python, LLM, Generative AI, RAG, PyTorch, Claude, TensorFlow, MLOps.
  • Frontend/API: API design, React, TypeScript, JavaScript, Node.js, Angular, REST API, Next.js.
  • Data/service: PostgreSQL, Kafka, MySQL, Redis, FastAPI, Elasticsearch, Django, RabbitMQ.
  • SQL/.NET/data warehouse: SQL, C#, .NET, Airflow, Spark, dbt, BigQuery, Redshift.

The most important network movement is not simply that AI terms appear. Python, LLM, generative AI, RAG, PyTorch, Claude, and MLOps form a coherent 2026 cluster. RAG, Claude, and Next.js are network entries under the strict company-spread threshold.

docs.title: Stack Breadth
docs.text:
Mean distinct technology indicators rise across every T30 junior and senior definition:

Definition Pooled 2024 Scraped 2026 Change J1 entry label 4.64 6.53 +1.89 J2 entry/associate 4.62 6.58 +1.96 J3 YOE <= 2 5.04 6.81 +1.76 J4 YOE <= 3 5.03 6.99 +1.96 S1 mid-senior/director 5.62 7.76 +2.14 S2 director 3.57 9.38 +5.81, sparse S3 title-keyword senior 5.16 7.05 +1.90 S4 YOE >= 5 5.33 7.82 +2.49

Density tells the confound. Tech density per 1,000 cleaned characters falls for most definitions because descriptions are longer. Example: J3 density falls from 6.03 to 4.55, and S1 falls from 7.28 to 5.42. This supports a "broader stack lists in longer/richer postings" reading rather than a clean claim that postings became denser per character.

docs.title: AI Integration
docs.text:
AI-tool/LLM rows are more stack-rich, but length explains part of the gap.

Period AI-tool/LLM mention n Mean tech count Mean non-AI tech count Mean non-AI tech density 2024 no 22,323 5.15 5.15 7.37 2024 yes 497 7.22 5.79 6.15 2026 no 32,379 6.60 6.60 5.58 2026 yes 8,502 9.78 7.63 5.23

In 2026, AI-tool/LLM postings mention about 1.0 more non-AI technologies than non-AI postings, but their non-AI density is lower because they are longer. AI is integrating into existing stacks, not replacing them, but the raw-count advantage is length-confounded.

Among 2026 AI-tool/LLM postings, the strongest traditional co-occurrence lifts are Python (+22.3 pp), API design (+18.2 pp), PyTorch (+12.3 pp), observability (+12.3 pp), and AWS (+10.4 pp) relative to non-AI rows.

docs.title: Structured Skills Baseline
docs.text:
Asaniczka structured skills validate the broad ranking of extracted technology signals. Across the 148 shared taxonomy technologies, structured-skill prevalence and description-extracted prevalence have Spearman rho = 0.817.

Top structured skills by posting count include Python (5,629), Java (4,679), SQL (3,656), AWS (3,441), JavaScript (3,129), Git (2,454), Agile (2,400), Linux (2,285), Kubernetes (2,229), Docker (2,130), C++ (2,010), C# (1,826), Azure (1,578), and Jira (1,499).

The divergences are instructive. Structured skills capture generic tags that descriptions often do not explicitly spell out: Agile is +31.0 pp higher in structured fields than extracted descriptions, DevOps +14.4 pp, unit testing +9.0 pp, machine learning +7.7 pp, SQL Server +6.1 pp, and Oracle +5.7 pp. The regex matrix is a good binary description screen, not a substitute for parsed skill taxonomies.

docs.title: Structured Seniority Skill Differences
docs.text:
Asaniczka structured skills are a useful but source-specific seniority baseline. Using FDR correction over skills appearing in at least 50 postings:

  • J1 vs S1 significant junior-higher skills include IoT, nginx, polygraph, sales, Excel, SVN, Pandas, and Apache. Several are odd enough to treat as source/template artifacts rather than a clean junior-skill taxonomy.
  • J3 vs S4 is more interpretable: low-YOE rows are higher on data pipelines (+2.09 pp), distributed computing (+1.55 pp), data structures (+2.12 pp), programming (+1.64 pp), and version control (+1.53 pp). S4 is higher on systems engineering, system integration, distributed microservices, people management, and U.S. citizenship.

This structured baseline supports the broader result that low-YOE rows are not simply "less technical"; they often list modern platform/data skills, while senior rows retain systems, integration, clearance, and people-management signals.

docs.title: Sensitivity Checks
docs.text:
Aggregator exclusion preserves the major movers. Pooled-2024 to scraped-2026 changes after excluding aggregators: Python +19.5 pp, CI/CD +16.6 pp, API design +13.5 pp, observability +12.7 pp, LLM +12.3 pp, Kubernetes +10.2 pp, AWS +11.0 pp, generative AI +6.7 pp.

Company cap 50 also preserves the major movers and often strengthens platform terms: CI/CD +18.6 pp, Python +18.6 pp, API design +15.7 pp, observability +12.8 pp, LLM +12.2 pp, AWS +11.7 pp, Kubernetes +10.3 pp.

Recommended SWE tier sensitivity, excluding swe_classification_tier = 'title_lookup_llm', preserves the same pattern: CI/CD +17.8 pp, Python +16.3 pp, API design +14.5 pp, observability +12.4 pp, LLM +12.2 pp, Kubernetes +8.6 pp.

Within-2024 calibration is built into trajectory. TypeScript and Azure rise in the scraped comparison but are not cleanly above arshkon-vs-asaniczka variation, so they should not be used as headline temporal claims without additional validation.

docs.title: Surprises & Unexpected Patterns
docs.text:
• Python reclusters into the 2026 AI/LLM neighborhood instead of remaining only a general backend/cloud language.
  • Testing does not grow with the rest of the stack. Testing category prevalence is roughly flat, 5.1% to 5.0%.
  • AI-tool/LLM postings list more traditional technologies, but lower non-AI tech density after length normalization.
  • Structured skills agree strongly with extracted tech rank order, but they expose systematic undercounting of generic practice tags in description text.
  • Some structured "junior" associations are odd and likely reflect source or template composition, not a clean junior skill profile.

docs.title: Caveats
docs.text:
• The shared tech matrix is regex-based over description_cleaned. It is appropriate for binary mention screens, but individual terms still need semantic validation before paper-grade prevalence claims.
  • description_cleaned mixes LLM-cleaned text and raw fallback. This is acceptable for binary mention recall, but density findings use cleaned-character length and should be interpreted cautiously for raw-fallback rows.
  • Asaniczka structured skills are 2024-only and source-specific. They validate extraction and seniority boundaries; they do not measure 2026 skills.
  • S2 director cells are sparse, and S3 inherits the T30 title-normalization caveat.
  • Community numeric IDs are not semantically stable across years; community labels in the report are based on top members and categories, not ID equality.

docs.title: Downstream Action Items
docs.text:
1. Gate 2 should verify the top calibrated movers: CI/CD, Python, API design, observability, LLM, Kubernetes, AWS, generative AI, and RAG.
  2. Use the AI/LLM + Python/platform cluster as the technology-evolution headline, not isolated AI keyword prevalence.
  3. Treat technology breadth growth as stack expansion in longer postings; do not claim density growth except where a density metric supports it.
  4. For interviews, ask whether AI requirements are screened as additions to existing stack expectations or as separate "nice-to-have" signals.
  5. T16/T28 should test whether the Python/AI/platform cluster is driven by new companies, specific domains, or within-company changes.

docs.title: T15. Semantic Similarity Landscape & Convergence Analysis
Headline
docs.text:
The corpus-level semantic convergence claim does not survive calibration. Under embeddings, junior-senior similarity rises only trivially from arshkon 2024 to scraped 2026 (+0.005), while the within-2024 arshkon-vs-asaniczka gap is larger (+0.022). Under TF-IDF/SVD, junior and senior postings become less similar from arshkon 2024 to scraped 2026 (-0.042). Raw-description TF-IDF also rejects convergence.

The dominant structure is domain/archetype, not seniority. In the bounded 9,054-row LLM-text sample, T09 archetype labels explain much more representation variance than seniority: eta-squared is 0.111 for archetype versus 0.007 for seniority in embeddings, and 0.132 versus 0.007 in TF-IDF/SVD.

docs.title: Methodology
docs.text:
Script: exploration/scripts/T15_semantic_similarity_landscape.py.

Inputs: swe_embeddings.npy, swe_embedding_index.parquet, swe_cleaned_text.parquet, swe_archetype_labels.parquet, the T30 seniority panel, and narrow metadata columns from data/unified.parquet. The script loaded shared embeddings only; it did not recompute embeddings, rerun BERTopic, rerun preprocessing, or make LLM calls.

Primary text frame: text_source = 'llm', length(description_cleaned) >= 100, linked to shared embedding rows. This gives 33,741 eligible LLM-text rows.

Bounded modeling sample: up to 2,000 rows per year x seniority_3level, deterministic by UID hash, including unknown seniority for the structural map. Final sample size is 9,054 rows.

Sample coverage:

Source-period Junior Mid Senior Unknown arshkon 2024 166 15 337 594 asaniczka 2024 197 24 1,663 1,406 scraped 2026-03 360 26 970 945 scraped 2026-04 241 25 1,030 1,055

Text-source coverage in the full shared artifact remains the main limitation: arshkon has 4,687 LLM rows and 4 raw-fallback rows; asaniczka has 17,037 LLM and 1,092 raw; scraped has 12,534 LLM and 28,347 raw. Therefore semantic findings describe the LLM-cleaned subset, especially for scraped 2026.

Representations:

  • Embeddings: shared all-MiniLM-L6-v2 embeddings, L2-normalized.
  • TF-IDF/SVD: TF-IDF over description_cleaned, unigram/bigram, 20,000 max features, reduced to 100 SVD components and L2-normalized.
  • Raw-text sensitivity: TF-IDF/SVD over sampled raw description from data/unified.parquet.

Centroid method: trimmed centroids remove the 10% most distant rows from each group before computing cosine similarity. Within-group dispersion uses an exact bounded formula from normalized vector sums, avoiding O(N^2) all-pairs matrices.

Figures:

  • exploration/figures/T15/umap_structural_map.png
  • exploration/figures/T15/pca_structural_map.png
  • exploration/figures/T15/centroid_similarity_heatmaps.png
  • exploration/figures/T15/nearest_neighbor_excess.png

t-SNE was skipped because UMAP/PCA were sufficient and the prior OOM made another heavy reducer an unnecessary risk.

docs.title: Domain Dominates Seniority
docs.text:
Variance explained by factor:

Representation Archetype Source Year Seniority Embedding 0.111 0.010 0.008 0.007 TF-IDF/SVD 0.132 0.014 0.013 0.007

This reproduces and strengthens T09's finding: natural posting structure is mostly technology/domain structure. Seniority is visible in some local neighborhoods, but it is not the main axis of semantic variation.

docs.title: Corpus-Level Convergence Is Rejected
docs.text:
Primary seniority_3level junior-senior centroid similarity:

Representation Arshkon 2024 Asaniczka 2024 Pooled 2024 Scraped 2026 Arshkon -> scraped Within-2024 calibration Verdict Embedding 0.954 0.976 0.974 0.959 +0.005 +0.022 reject TF-IDF/SVD 0.884 0.939 0.934 0.842 -0.042 +0.055 reject

The embedding result is a tiny positive shift only if arshkon is the 2024 comparator. It disappears against pooled 2024: scraped 2026 is lower than pooled 2024 by -0.015. The TF-IDF result points the other way, with scraped junior and senior postings farther apart than arshkon junior and senior postings.

Raw-description TF-IDF also rejects convergence: arshkon junior-senior similarity is 0.948, scraped 2026 is 0.913, for a -0.035 shift. That matters because a boilerplate-heavy raw representation does not rescue the convergence story.

docs.title: T30 Panel
docs.text:
The T30 seniority panel gives the same practical verdict.

  • J1/S1 embedding similarity rises by +0.005, but within-2024 calibration is +0.022, so it rejects.
