  • J1/S1 TF-IDF falls by -0.042, so it rejects directly.
  • J1/S4 embedding is essentially flat (-0.002), and TF-IDF falls (-0.055).
  • J2 behaves like J1.
  • J3/J4 low-YOE definitions show small positive embedding shifts against S1/S4, but those shifts are below within-2024 calibration. TF-IDF is flat or negative.
  • The only apparent embedding passes are J3/S3 and J4/S3, but S3 is the artifact-prone title-normalized senior proxy and is thin in the bounded sample: arshkon S3 n = 18 and scraped S3 n = 15. I do not treat those as substantive evidence.

Conclusion: no seniority operationalization supports a robust corpus-level junior-senior convergence claim.

docs.title: Sensitivities
docs.text:
The primary rejection survives the required sensitivity checks.

Spec Embedding shift TF-IDF/SVD shift Verdict Primary all rows +0.005 -0.042 reject Exclude aggregators +0.003 -0.054 reject Company cap 50 +0.003 -0.042 reject Exclude title_lookup_llm tier +0.005 -0.048 reject Raw-description TF-IDF n/a -0.035 reject

Source restriction is decisive. Arshkon-only gives the most favorable embedding comparison, but its +0.005 shift is smaller than the +0.022 arshkon-vs-asaniczka calibration gap. Pooled 2024 to scraped 2026 is negative under both representations.

docs.title: Within-Group Dispersion
docs.text:
Groups become more internally similar in 2026, especially in embeddings:

Group Embedding avg pairwise cosine 2024 2026 Junior 0.492 0.520 Senior 0.494 0.543 Unknown 0.451 0.503

TF-IDF/SVD shows the same direction but smaller magnitudes. This is not junior-senior convergence; it is within-group consolidation. A plausible interpretation is that 2026 postings are more templated/domain-concentrated, or that the LLM-cleaned scraped subset is narrower than the full scraped corpus.

docs.title: Nearest Neighbors
docs.text:
For each 2026 junior-definition query, the script finds 5 nearest 2024 neighbors in a company-capped 8,000-row candidate pool and reports excess over the candidate base rate.

Senior-neighbor excess:

Query definition Embedding TF-IDF J1 entry label +3.2 pp -8.6 pp J2 entry/associate +4.2 pp -7.2 pp J3 YOE <= 2 +12.4 pp +4.6 pp J4 YOE <= 3 +12.6 pp +0.4 pp

This is the most nuanced semantic finding. Label-based 2026 entry rows are enriched for 2024 junior neighbors under both representations: J1 junior-neighbor excess is +8.2 pp in embeddings and +7.0 pp in TF-IDF. Low-YOE rows, however, are more senior-neighbor-heavy, especially under embeddings. That supports the broader Wave 2 puzzle: low-YOE postings and explicit entry labels are not the same construct.

docs.title: Archetype-Stratified Convergence
docs.text:
Within-domain convergence is mostly thin or representation-sensitive.

  • Enterprise Application Support passes the embedding calibration check by a small margin (+0.016 arshkon-to-scraped versus +0.015 within-2024), but TF-IDF rejects it (+0.012 versus +0.047 calibration).
  • AI LLM Platforms has a large positive embedding shift (+0.052), but arshkon junior n = 14 and within-2024 calibration is unavailable, so it is descriptive only.
  • Several other archetypes have arshkon junior cells below 10, making calibration impossible.

The right next step is not to claim within-domain convergence from T15; it is to test domain-specific requirement migration with more targeted, validated features.

docs.title: Outliers
docs.text: Outliers are mostly domain pockets, not seniority mistakes. Examples include embedded/C++ junior roles, defense-clearance junior roles, Android unknown roles, mainframe unknown roles, AI-heavy senior roles, and Google/large-employer platform postings. This aligns with the variance finding: unusual technology/domain content moves a posting farther from its seniority centroid than the seniority label itself.

docs.title: Surprises & Unexpected Patterns
docs.text:
• The embedding representation is so high-similarity at the centroid level that the favorable convergence estimate is only +0.005 and is swamped by source calibration.
  • TF-IDF/SVD is more discriminating and points toward divergence, not convergence.
  • 2026 rows are more internally homogeneous within seniority groups, even though junior and senior groups are not converging.
  • J1 entry-labeled rows have junior-neighbor enrichment, while J3/J4 low-YOE rows have senior-neighbor enrichment. This is a stronger semantic version of the label-vs-YOE split found in T08/T30.
  • Domain/archetype explains about 15-18x as much variance as seniority in embeddings and TF-IDF.

docs.title: Caveats
docs.text:
• Scraped 2026 LLM-cleaned coverage is only 12,534 rows out of 40,881 default LinkedIn SWE rows. Semantic claims are LLM-subset evidence.
  • The bounded sample keeps memory stable but makes tiny groups thinner. S2 and S3 should be treated as descriptive only.
  • T30 S3 inherits the known title-normalization caveat from Wave 1; apparent S3 convergence is not a substantive senior-role finding.
  • UMAP/PCA are communication aids, not estimators. The tables drive the conclusions.
  • Nearest-neighbor results are representation-sensitive and should be manually spot-checked before being used as paper-grade evidence.

docs.title: Downstream Action Items
docs.text:
1. Gate 2 should reject a generic "junior postings became semantically like senior postings" claim.
  2. Keep the lower-YOE/entry-label split as a central mechanism: J1 behaves differently from J3/J4 in nearest-neighbor structure.
  3. T20/T28 should test convergence within specific archetypes using validated requirement families, not broad corpus centroids.
  4. Expand scraped LLM-cleaned coverage before making final semantic-landscape claims in the paper.
  5. Use T15 figures to communicate that the posting space is organized by domain and technology first, with seniority as a weaker secondary layer.

docs.title: Headline Finding
docs.text:
Among companies with at least 3 SWE LinkedIn postings in both arshkon 2024 and scraped 2026, the AI/tool and skill-breadth expansion is mostly a within-company change, not just a reshuffling toward different employers. The same common firms moved from 3.74% to 23.23% broad-AI mention prevalence and from 2.23% to 19.80% AI-tool-specific prevalence. In the midpoint decomposition, 91-92% of the absolute AI/tool component is within company.

The junior story is the opposite: explicit entry labels and YOE proxies diverge. In the arshkon-to-scraped common-company panel, J1 entry share fell from 3.46% to 3.02%, while J3 low-YOE share rose from 14.42% to 21.05%. The low-YOE increase is mostly between-company reweighting, not uniform within-company relabeling.

docs.title: Methodology
docs.text:
Default row filter: source_platform='linkedin', is_english=true, date_flag='ok', is_swe=true.

Company panel:

  • Primary overlap: companies with at least 3 SWE postings in both kaggle_arshkon 2024 and scraped LinkedIn 2026. This produced 240 companies.
  • Source sensitivity: companies with at least 3 postings in pooled 2024 (kaggle_arshkon + kaggle_asaniczka) and scraped 2026. This produced 589 companies.
  • Company grouping uses company_name_canonical; aggregators are retained in the primary panel and excluded in a sensitivity.

Metric definitions and denominators:

  • Broad AI prevalence: any shared tech-matrix broad AI column used by V1/T08, including mcp; denominator is all eligible postings in the relevant company/period.
  • AI-tool-specific prevalence: shared taxonomy category='ai_tool', excluding mcp; denominator is all eligible postings in the relevant company/period.
  • Tech breadth: mean count of all shared tech-matrix indicators; denominator is all eligible postings.
  • Platform tech breadth: mean count of shared taxonomy cloud, devops, ops, architecture, and tooling columns; denominator is all eligible postings.
  • Requirement breadth and org-scope counts: T11 regex-derived features from posting_complexity_features.parquet, restricted to text_source='llm'; denominator is LLM-labeled text rows only.
  • J1/J2 use seniority_final; J3/J4 use yoe_extracted; YOE-share denominators are postings with non-null extracted YOE.

No new keyword regex was introduced. AI/tool indicators, org-scope indicators, and requirement-breadth components reuse Wave 2/V1-validated artifacts. T22 has not run yet, so org/scope and management-like terms should be read as posting-language signals, not screened-requirement proof.

docs.title: Evidence
docs.text:
Eligible default LinkedIn SWE rows:

Source Eligible n Companies LLM-text n Broad AI AI-tool strict Mean tech count arshkon 2024 4,691 2,298 4,687 4.82% 3.05% 5.31 asaniczka 2024 18,129 4,948 17,037 3.48% 1.80% 5.17 scraped 2026 40,881 7,940 12,534 23.70% 20.19% 7.26

docs.title: Common-Company Decomposition
docs.text:
Primary arshkon-to-scraped panel:

Metric Companies used 2024 2026 Change Within-company Between-company J1 entry share 240 3.46% 3.02% -0.44 pp -0.95 pp +0.50 pp J3 low-YOE share 222 14.42% 21.05% +6.63 pp +0.56 pp +6.07 pp Broad AI prevalence 240 3.74% 23.23% +19.49 pp +17.73 pp +1.76 pp AI-tool strict prevalence 240 2.23% 19.80% +17.57 pp +16.16 pp +1.41 pp Raw description length 240 3,473 5,320 +1,847 chars +935 +913 Requirement breadth, LLM text 223 7.34 9.25 +1.91 +2.23 -0.33 Tech count 240 5.45 7.19 +1.74 +2.06 -0.32 Platform tech count 240 2.01 2.47 +0.46 +0.64 -0.18

Interpretation: AI/tool prevalence, tech breadth, platform breadth, and requirement breadth are not mainly new-firm composition stories inside the overlap panel. The description-length increase is split about half within-company and half reweighting.

docs.title: Trajectory Clusters
docs.text:
K-means on standardized company-level deltas produced 5 descriptive trajectories:

Trajectory Companies Main pattern Stack-breadth expanders 97 Small J1 change, modest J3 growth, +10.0 pp AI-tool strict, +3.94 tech count, +4.17 requirement breadth Low-YOE growth, tool-light shift 66 J1 and J3 up, but tech count and LLM requirement breadth down AI-heavy scope expanders 51 J1/J3 down or flat, but +37.8 pp AI-tool strict and +3.01 LLM requirement breadth Low-YOE retreat with AI broadening 14 Large low-YOE decline with AI/tool and breadth growth Label/YOE divergence spike 12 J1 down sharply, J3 up sharply, AI/tool and breadth also up

The clusters should be treated as exploratory typology, not causal classes. Cleaned-text deltas are imputed for clustering when a company lacks LLM rows in one period; the raw feature table preserves the missingness.

docs.title: New 2026 Entrants
docs.text:
Against any 2024 company appearance, scraped 2026 has 5,916 new companies contributing 18,317 postings, or 44.8% of scraped LinkedIn SWE postings.

New entrants differ from returning firms in ways that complicate a simple "new AI employers explain everything" story:

  • Broad AI prevalence is higher for new entrants: 25.44% vs 22.28%.
  • AI-tool strict prevalence is higher: 21.79% vs 18.90%.
  • Mean tech count is nearly the same: 7.28 vs 7.24.
  • LLM-text requirement breadth is nearly the same: 9.39 vs 9.38.
  • J3 low-YOE share is lower for new entrants: 14.59% vs 19.10%.
  • Aggregator share is higher: 19.97% vs 13.93%.

Top new entrants by posting volume include Jobs via Dice (1,711 postings, all aggregator), Haystack (232), PayPal (137), Akkodis Group Nordics (134), and TalentAlly (126). AI-native firms show up but do not dominate volume; for example Anthropic has 77 postings with 79.2% broad-AI and 75.3% AI-tool strict prevalence.

docs.title: Aggregators Versus Direct Employers
docs.text:
In scraped 2026, aggregators account for 6,800 of 40,881 eligible postings. They do not create the AI expansion:

  • Direct employers: 24.27% broad AI, 20.63% AI-tool strict.
  • Aggregators: 20.82% broad AI, 18.00% AI-tool strict.
  • Aggregators have lower low-YOE share: 13.19% vs 17.85%.
  • Aggregators have slightly higher mean tech count: 7.44 vs 7.23.

Aggregator exclusion leaves the main AI/tool and requirement-breadth directions intact.

docs.title: Archetype Dimension
docs.text:
For overlap-company rows with T09 labels and LLM text, broad AI increased in every archetype. AI-tool strict increases were largest in AI LLM Platforms (+29.8 pp), then Large Employer Data Platforms (+17.4 pp), Product Backend Engineering (+12.2 pp), and Java Dotnet Web Services (+8.3 pp).

Requirement breadth rose in 7 of 8 archetypes; the exception is Large Employer Data Platforms (-1.71 signals), despite its AI/tool growth. This is a useful contradiction: not every AI/domain shift is a generic breadth increase.

docs.title: Seniority Panel
docs.text:
Common-company arshkon-to-scraped panel:

Variant Definition Change Within vs between Verdict J1 seniority_final='entry' -0.44 pp within -0.95 pp, between +0.50 pp Down J2 entry/associate -0.53 pp within -1.08 pp, between +0.55 pp Down J3 yoe_extracted<=2 among YOE-known +6.63 pp within +0.56 pp, between +6.07 pp Up J4 yoe_extracted<=3 among YOE-known +10.32 pp within +1.55 pp, between +8.77 pp Up

Pooled 2024 flips J1/J2 upward (+1.51 pp and +1.53 pp), while J3/J4 remain up. The agreement verdict is therefore mixed by construct and source: explicit labels decline in the arshkon-only common panel, but low-YOE shares rise, and pooled 2024 label shares rise.

docs.title: Sensitivity Checks
docs.text:
• Aggregator exclusion: AI/tool, tech breadth, platform breadth, and requirement breadth remain directionally stable. In arshkon-only common companies, broad AI changes +19.84 pp after exclusion; AI-tool strict changes +17.79 pp.
  • Source restriction: material for seniority. Pooled 2024 gives J1 up, while arshkon-only gives J1 down. Do not cite a single company-level junior-share direction without specifying the 2024 source.
  • LLM coverage: scraped has 12,534 LLM-text rows out of 40,881 eligible rows. Requirement breadth, org scope, and cleaned length claims are LLM-labeled subset claims.
  • SWE-tier exclusion: overall pooled headline shifts are directionally stable when excluding title_lookup_llm rows.

docs.title: Surprises
docs.text:
• The strongest within-company movement is not junior reduction; it is AI/tool and stack breadth growth.
  • New entrants are somewhat more AI-heavy, but their requirement breadth is almost identical to returning firms.
  • Low-YOE expansion in the common-company panel is mostly between-company reweighting, suggesting a changing mix of overlap employers rather than uniform relabeling inside each firm.
  • Domain labels matter: one large-employer data-platform archetype shows AI growth with lower requirement breadth.

docs.title: Evidence Strength
docs.text:
Strong for company-level AI/tool and tech-breadth expansion: full default SWE frame, 240 arshkon-scraped overlap companies, stable aggregator sensitivity, and validated shared tech indicators.

Moderate for requirement breadth: strong direction but restricted to LLM-cleaned rows, with scraped LLM coverage around 31%.

Weak-to-mixed for company-level junior-share direction: J1/J2 and J3/J4 disagree, and source restriction changes the label-based result.

docs.title: Data Quality Caveats
docs.text:
• Company canonicalization can merge or split firms imperfectly, especially aggregators and subsidiaries.
  • company_industry is null for asaniczka and therefore less useful in pooled 2024.
  • T11/T22 scope caveat: org-scope and requirement-breadth metrics identify posting language, not whether employers actually screen those requirements.
  • Broad AI and AI-tool strict are separate constructs; broad AI includes mcp, while AI-tool strict excludes it.

docs.title: Action Items
docs.text:
1. Treat company-level AI/tool and tech-breadth expansion as a strong mechanism result for the paper.
  2. Do not lead with "common companies reduced junior hiring"; present the label-vs-YOE divergence instead.
  3. Use T22 to test whether within-company breadth growth is screened, preferred, aspirational, or template language.
  4. In analysis-phase models, include company fixed effects or company reweighting for AI/tool and breadth outcomes; for junior outcomes, report J1-J4 separately.

docs.title: Headline Finding
docs.text:
The AI/tool shift is geographically broad rather than confined to a few tech hubs. Across the 26 metros with at least 50 SWE LinkedIn postings in both pooled 2024 and scraped 2026, broad-AI prevalence increased in all 26 metros, with a mean change of +17.6 pp. AI-tool-specific prevalence also increased in all 26 metros, with a mean change of +16.1 pp.

Tech hubs have somewhat larger AI/tool increases, but the difference is not the main story: heuristic tech hubs average +19.8 pp broad AI versus +16.7 pp in other metros. AI change is essentially uncorrelated with 2024 metro posting volume and with entry/low-YOE share changes.

docs.title: Methodology
docs.text:
Default row filter: source_platform='linkedin', is_english=true, date_flag='ok', is_swe=true.

Metro rollup rules:

  • Use metro_area, not scraper search metro metadata.
  • Exclude is_multi_location=true; do not expand multi-location rows.
  • Primary comparison uses pooled 2024 (kaggle_arshkon + kaggle_asaniczka) versus scraped 2026.
  • Eligible metro threshold: at least 50 SWE postings in both periods. This produced 26 metros.

Metric definitions and denominators:

  • Broad AI prevalence: any V1/T08 broad AI tech-matrix column, including mcp; denominator is all eligible metro-period postings.
  • AI-tool-specific prevalence: shared taxonomy category='ai_tool', excluding mcp; denominator is all eligible metro-period postings.
  • J1/J2 use seniority_final; denominator is all eligible metro-period postings.
  • J3/J4 use yoe_extracted; denominator is postings with non-null extracted YOE.
  • Requirement breadth and org-scope counts use T11 features restricted to text_source='llm'; denominator is LLM-text rows.
  • Raw length is median description_length; cleaned length is median T11 char_len among LLM-text rows.
  • Tech diversity is the count of shared tech indicators with at least one mention in the metro-period.

docs.title: Sample Sizes And Exclusions
docs.text:
Multi-location rows excluded from metro rollups:

Source Eligible n Multi-location excluded Excluded share Non-multi metro candidates arshkon 2024 4,691 116 2.47% 2,774 asaniczka 2024 18,129 894 4.93% 12,884 scraped 2026 40,881 3,090 7.56% 30,560

The 26 eligible metros contain 13,454 pooled-2024 postings and 30,560 scraped-2026 postings. LLM-text coverage inside those metros is 12,799 rows in 2024 and 9,198 rows in 2026, so requirement-breadth and org-scope geography are labeled-subset findings.

docs.title: Metro Results
docs.text:
AI increases are positive everywhere:

Metric Mean change Median change Minimum Maximum Positive metros Broad AI prevalence +17.6 pp +17.8 pp +8.7 pp +27.4 pp 26 / 26 AI-tool strict prevalence +16.1 pp +16.4 pp +6.7 pp +24.1 pp 26 / 26 Requirement breadth, LLM text +2.14 +2.20 +0.53 +3.48 26 / 26 Mean tech count +2.00 +2.02 +0.31 +3.21 26 / 26

Largest broad-AI increases:

Metro 2024 n 2026 n Broad AI change AI-tool strict change Requirement breadth change Tampa Bay 214 290 +27.4 pp +24.1 pp +3.48 New York City 70 2,043 +25.3 pp +23.1 pp +3.33 Salt Lake City 327 1,724 +25.0 pp +23.6 pp +1.80 San Francisco Bay Area 1,730 5,012 +24.0 pp +23.9 pp +1.24 Seattle 762 2,201 +23.9 pp +22.8 pp +2.32

Smallest broad-AI increases are still positive: Pittsburgh +8.7 pp, Detroit +9.4 pp, Denver +10.7 pp, Columbus +12.1 pp, and Los Angeles +12.5 pp.

docs.title: Concentration And Correlation Tests
docs.text:
Heuristic tech hubs: San Francisco Bay Area, Seattle, New York City, Boston, Austin, Raleigh-Durham-Chapel Hill, San Diego, Los Angeles.

Group Broad AI mean change AI-tool strict mean change Requirement breadth mean change Tech-hub heuristic metros +19.8 pp +18.1 pp +2.01 Other metros +16.7 pp +15.2 pp +2.20 Top 2024 volume quartile +18.5 pp +16.8 pp +2.28 Lower-volume metros +17.4 pp +15.8 pp +2.10

Metro-level correlations:

  • Broad-AI change vs J1 entry-share change: Pearson r = -0.19, p = 0.34.
  • Broad-AI change vs J3 low-YOE share change: r = 0.04, p = 0.86.
  • AI-tool strict change vs J3 low-YOE share change: r = -0.02, p = 0.92.
  • Broad-AI change vs requirement-breadth change: r = 0.44, p = 0.026.
  • Broad-AI change vs 2024 posting volume: r = -0.02, p = 0.93.

Interpretation: AI/tool growth travels with requirement-breadth growth, not with a clear metro-level junior/low-YOE decline.

docs.title: Seniority Panel
docs.text:
Primary pooled-2024 metro panel:

Variant Definition Mean change Median change Positive metros Verdict J1 seniority_final='entry' +1.12 pp +1.47 pp 20 / 26 Mostly up J2 entry/associate +1.06 pp +1.33 pp 20 / 26 Mostly up J3 yoe_extracted<=2 +4.95 pp +3.89 pp 20 / 26 Mostly up J4 yoe_extracted<=3 +6.47 pp +5.30 pp 20 / 26 Mostly up

Source restriction materially changes label-based seniority geography. In the arshkon-only 2024 sensitivity, only 18 metros meet the >=50 threshold; J1 and J2 average changes are negative (-0.77 pp and -0.94 pp), while broad AI and AI-tool strict remain positive in all 18 metros. This reinforces the Gate 2 warning: geography does not rescue a simple junior-share story.

docs.title: Archetype / Domain Composition
docs.text:
T09 archetype labels exist only for LLM-text rows. Within the 26 eligible metros, archetype-labeled rows total 12,586 in 2024 and 9,078 in 2026.

A simple archetype-mix adjustment suggests most metro AI variation is domain-composition aligned rather than a metro-specific shock. Among labeled rows:

  • Mean actual broad-AI delta: +16.87 pp.
  • Mean predicted delta from metro archetype mix and national archetype-period AI rates: +16.84 pp.
  • Mean residual delta: +0.02 pp.
  • Median absolute residual: 1.85 pp.

This does not prove causality; it says that once the LLM-labeled archetype mix is considered, little systematic metro residual remains.

docs.title: Remote Work
docs.text: Remote work is not usable in this artifact. Scraped 2026 has 40,881 eligible LinkedIn SWE postings, but is_remote_inferred is zero for all rows, including the 30,560 metro-rollup rows. Since 2024 remote flags are known artifact zeros and 2026 is also zero here, this field needs a scraper/location-stage audit before any remote-work claim.

docs.title: Sensitivity Checks
docs.text:
Aggregator exclusion:

  • AI findings are stable: broad-AI mean delta +17.5 pp versus +17.6 pp primary; AI-tool strict +15.9 pp versus +16.1 pp primary.
  • Seniority metrics are sensitive at the metro level: 9 of 26 metros have material J1 or J3 changes under aggregator exclusion.
  • Requirement breadth is mostly stable, though 4 of 26 metros cross the >30% material-change threshold.

Company cap at 20 postings per company per metro-period:

  • AI findings are stable: broad-AI mean delta +17.8 pp; AI-tool strict +16.2 pp.
  • Requirement breadth and tech count are stable in almost all metros.
  • J3 low-YOE share is more sensitive: 6 of 26 metros are material under the cap.

Arshkon-only 2024 source restriction:

  • AI remains positive in all 18 eligible metros.
  • Label-based junior shares turn mostly negative.
  • Low-YOE shares become mixed.

docs.title: Surprises
docs.text:
• New York City has only 70 pooled-2024 eligible postings but more than 2,000 scraped-2026 postings, so its large AI increase is partly a coverage/composition warning.
  • Tampa Bay has the largest broad-AI increase despite not being in the tech-hub heuristic group.
  • Remote inference is all zero in scraped 2026, not just 2024.
  • AI growth is more correlated with requirement-breadth growth than with tech-count growth or seniority shifts.

docs.title: Evidence Strength
docs.text:
Strong for broad geographic AI/tool expansion: all 26 eligible metros move upward, and aggregator/company-cap sensitivities are stable.

Moderate for requirement-breadth geography: all metros move upward, but the result is restricted to LLM-text rows and scraped LLM coverage is limited.

Weak-to-mixed for seniority geography: pooled 2024 shows mostly upward junior/low-YOE shares, but arshkon-only source restriction reverses or weakens label-based patterns.

Not usable for remote work until the all-zero 2026 field is audited.

docs.title: Data Quality Caveats
docs.text:
• Metro assignment excludes multi-location postings and unresolved locations; this is correct for rollups but changes denominators.
  • search_metro_name is not used; only metro_area is used.
  • Asaniczka and arshkon have different geographic and company coverage, which affects pooled-2024 metro baselines.
  • Archetype-composition checks are LLM-labeled subset analyses.
  • The tech-hub grouping is a heuristic, not an official labor-market classification.

docs.title: Action Items
docs.text:
1. Use geography as supporting evidence for broad AI/tool diffusion, not as a lead mechanism.
  2. Keep seniority geography secondary and always report arshkon-only sensitivity.
  3. Audit is_remote_inferred before any remote-work analysis.
  4. In formal models, include metro fixed effects or metro-level controls for domain mix, but expect the AI/tool signal to remain broadly positive.

docs.title: Headline Finding
docs.text:
Wave 2's AI/platform/workflow expansion is SWE-amplified, not SWE-specific. The same direction appears in SWE-adjacent roles, often at nearly the same magnitude, while controls move only modestly. This weakens a paper frame based on uniquely SWE restructuring, but supports a stronger frame: software-adjacent technical work is absorbing AI/tool/platform requirements much faster than the broader control labor market.

Key result, default LinkedIn / English / date-ok frame:

Metric SWE 2024 -> 2026 Adjacent 2024 -> 2026 Control 2024 -> 2026 SWE-control DiD Verdict Broad AI, raw binary 12.5% -> 39.7% (+27.2 pp) 11.8% -> 38.8% (+27.0 pp) 0.5% -> 3.1% (+2.6 pp) +24.5 pp SWE-amplified, adjacent-parallel AI-tool, raw binary 2.2% -> 24.0% (+21.8 pp) 2.1% -> 21.2% (+19.1 pp) 0.01% -> 0.8% (+0.8 pp) +21.0 pp SWE-amplified AI-domain, raw binary 11.9% -> 29.0% (+17.1 pp) 11.6% -> 32.8% (+21.2 pp) 0.5% -> 2.6% (+2.1 pp) +14.9 pp adjacent even stronger Bounded tech count, raw mean 5.00 -> 7.36 (+2.36) 3.06 -> 5.37 (+2.31) 0.22 -> 0.35 (+0.14) +2.22 SWE-amplified Requirement breadth, LLM-labeled 8.15 -> 10.71 (+2.56) 6.39 -> 9.31 (+2.93) 2.21 -> 2.92 (+0.70) +1.86 SWE-amplified, adjacent stronger Org-scope prevalence, LLM-labeled 46.5% -> 61.9% (+15.5 pp) 49.6% -> 67.5% (+17.8 pp) 20.4% -> 31.8% (+11.4 pp) +4.1 pp partly field-wide

Interpretation: the Wave 2 story should not be written as "SWE alone changed." It is more defensible as an AI-era expansion in software-producing and software-adjacent technical roles, with controls showing a smaller background rise.

docs.title: Methodology
docs.text:
Frame: source_platform='linkedin' AND is_english=true AND date_flag='ok', grouped as SWE (is_swe), SWE-adjacent (is_swe_adjacent), and controls (is_control). Eligible rows: 242,854 total in the feature artifact.

Text and metric definitions:

  • Broad AI: raw-description binary mention of AI-tool or AI-domain families, excluding mcp from the primary definition.
  • AI-tool: LLMs, generative AI, coding assistants, model APIs, agents, prompt engineering, model fine-tuning, evals, RAG, LLM frameworks, and vector databases. After validation, the script tightened false-prone subpatterns for bare Claude, legal LLM, and generic non-model "fine-tuning."
  • AI-domain: machine learning, deep learning, NLP, computer vision, MLOps, PyTorch/TensorFlow, Hugging Face, and AI/ML/artificial-intelligence domain language; generic NumPy/Pandas/SciPy/scikit-learn are not included.
  • Tech count: a bounded high-frequency cross-occupation taxonomy, not the full T14 148-indicator SWE matrix.
  • Org-scope and requirement breadth: LLM-labeled cleaned text only. Denominator is llm_extraction_coverage='labeled'.
  • TF-IDF boundary sample: 200 SWE and 200 SWE-adjacent LLM-cleaned descriptions per period, 800 rows total.

Artifacts:

  • exploration/tables/T18/metric_by_occupation_period.csv
  • exploration/tables/T18/difference_in_differences.csv
  • exploration/tables/T18/seniority_panel_changes.csv
  • exploration/tables/T18/boundary_tfidf_similarity.csv
  • exploration/tables/T18/adjacent_role_profiles.csv
  • exploration/figures/T18/ with four figures

docs.title: Sample Sizes
docs.text:
Group 2024 eligible 2024 LLM-labeled 2026 eligible 2026 LLM-labeled SWE 22,820 21,724 40,881 12,534 SWE-adjacent 11,816 11,804 11,592 11,521 Control 113,955 29,230 41,790 8,967

LLM coverage is the main denominator caveat: adjacent text coverage is nearly complete, while scraped SWE and control text-sensitive metrics are subset evidence.

docs.title: Boundary Shift
docs.text:
The generic SWE-adjacent boundary does not clearly converge toward SWE in TF-IDF space.

Similarity 2024 2026 Change SWE-adjacent centroid cosine 0.838 0.817 -0.021 Mean pairwise cosine 0.066 0.074 +0.007

The centroid result moves apart, while the pairwise mean rises slightly. I treat this as mixed/no generic convergence, consistent with T15's rejection of broad semantic convergence.

Terms moving into adjacent descriptions include python, cloud, automation, test, familiarity, cross functional, apis, tooling, exposure, sdlc, and github. These look like workflow/platform and engineering-practice migration, not wholesale occupation collapse.

docs.title: Adjacent Roles
docs.text:
Some adjacent families are nearly SWE-like on the measured requirement surface:

2026 adjacent family n Broad AI AI-tool AI-domain Tech count gap vs SWE Breadth gap vs SWE Data scientist / ML 2,558 83.2% 40.5% 79.8% -0.22 +0.75 Data engineer 138 43.5% 24.6% 31.9% -0.66 +0.48 Product/solutions/support 161 28.6% 21.1% 18.0% -1.79 -0.41

Data scientist/ML and data engineer are the closest to SWE by these metrics, but they are not "indistinguishable" in title/domain terms. The better claim is boundary thickening: specific adjacent families now share AI/platform requirements with SWE.

docs.title: Seniority Panel
docs.text:
SWE junior definitions all rise, matching Wave 2's label/YOE ambiguity rather than junior elimination.

Variant Definition SWE change Verdict J1 seniority_final='entry' +1.46 pp Up J2 entry/associate +1.41 pp Up J3 yoe_extracted<=2, YOE-known denom +5.98 pp Up J4 yoe_extracted<=3, YOE-known denom +8.12 pp Up

SWE senior definitions are mixed: S1 mid-senior/director -16.25 pp, S2 director +0.26 pp, S3 senior-title regex -14.42 pp, S4 YOE>=5 -7.50 pp. Agreement verdict: junior unanimous up; senior mixed because director-only rises while broader senior definitions fall.

Controls behave differently: label-based junior rises slightly, but YOE-based low-experience shares fall sharply. This is another sign that occupation-specific seniority labels and YOE floors are not interchangeable.

docs.title: Sensitivity Checks
docs.text:
The core SWE-control DiD survives required sensitivities.

Metric Base DiD Exclude aggregators Exclude SWE title_lookup_llm Company cap 50 Broad AI +24.5 pp +26.4 pp +24.8 pp +22.4 pp AI-tool +21.0 pp +21.4 pp +21.4 pp +20.9 pp Tech count +2.22 +2.40 +2.14 +2.41 Requirement breadth +1.86 +2.09 +1.76 +2.16 Org-scope prevalence +4.1 pp +4.9 pp +4.1 pp +3.4 pp

Within-2024 calibration strengthens the AI/tech interpretation: arshkon-to-asaniczka differences are tiny or opposite-signed for AI and tech count, while cross-period DiD is large. Org-scope is less clean because controls also rise.

docs.title: Evidence Strength
docs.text:
Strong:

  • AI-tool and broad-AI growth is sharply larger in SWE and adjacent than controls.
  • Bounded tech count and LLM-labeled requirement breadth rise in SWE and adjacent, with sensitivity robustness.
  • Adjacent roles, especially data scientist/ML and data engineering, share much of the 2026 AI/platform surface.

Moderate:

  • Boundary migration terms from TF-IDF are discovery evidence only, based on capped samples.
  • Org-scope language is real but partly field-wide.

Weak/rejected:

  • Generic SWE-adjacent semantic convergence. Centroid similarity falls, and pairwise similarity rises only slightly.

docs.title: Data Quality Caveats
docs.text:
• The T18 tech count is a bounded cross-occupation taxonomy, not T14's full SWE tech matrix.
  • Requirement breadth and org-scope metrics are LLM-labeled subset evidence.
  • Raw binary AI prevalence can include company/background AI mentions, not only hard requirements. Manual review of 100 saved AI contexts found semantic AI-topic matches after tightening patterns, but requirement force is not parsed.
  • mcp is tracked separately and excluded from primary AI due ambiguity.
  • The full T18 script should not be rerun casually; the final feature artifact is readable and nonzero, but the advisor requested no further full reruns.

docs.title: Action Items
docs.text:
1. Frame the paper story as SWE-amplified technical-role restructuring, not uniquely SWE-only restructuring.
  2. Treat controls as a background trend check: they move on length and org-scope, but not much on AI/tool/tech/breadth.
  3. Follow up on data scientist/ML and data engineer boundaries in T20/T28 if boundary mechanisms become central.
  4. For paper-grade AI-tool rates, keep the tightened patterns and continue excluding mcp from primary AI.

docs.title: T24: Hypothesis Generation For Formal Analysis
Scope
docs.text:
This report synthesizes existing exploration reports and gate memos only. No new data analysis, preprocessing, parquet inspection, or LLM calls were run for T24.

The hypotheses below are designed for the formal analysis phase. Each separates the construct from the expected empirical direction, the current exploratory evidence, required robustness checks, and falsification conditions. Several hypotheses intentionally contradict the original junior-collapse framing because the completed evidence does not support that as the lead story.

docs.title: H1. Returning-company AI/tool expansion
docs.text:
Construct: Within-company change in employer posting language for broad AI and specific AI tools.

Expected direction: AI/tool prevalence increases substantially from 2024 to 2026 within returning SWE employers, with most of the absolute change attributable to within-company change rather than reweighting toward different companies.

Current evidence: T16 reports that 240 arshkon-to-scraped overlap companies moved from 3.74% to 23.23% broad-AI prevalence and from 2.23% to 19.80% AI-tool-specific prevalence. The midpoint decomposition assigns 91-92% of the AI/tool change to within-company movement. V1 verified the broad-AI prevalence jump in the shared tech matrix.

Required robustness checks:

  • Company fixed effects or common-company decomposition, with company-clustered uncertainty.
  • Exclusion of aggregators and capped-company row weights.
  • Arshkon-only versus pooled-2024 baseline, reported separately.
  • Broad AI, AI-tool-specific, and AI-domain specifications separated.
  • Ambiguous terms excluded or shown separately, especially mcp.
  • Sensitivity to company-name canonicalization and subsidiaries.
  • Requirement-force parsing once T22-style evidence exists.

Would falsify: The AI/tool increase disappears or becomes small under company fixed effects; the within-company component falls below the between-company component; the effect depends on ambiguous broad-AI terms; or manual/section validation shows the increase is mostly company background rather than role requirement language.

docs.title: H2. Technical-work skill-surface expansion, not SWE-only restructuring
docs.text:
Construct: Differential expansion of AI/tool, technology, and requirement breadth in SWE, SWE-adjacent technical roles, and controls.

Expected direction: SWE and SWE-adjacent roles both show large increases, while controls rise only modestly. SWE may be amplified relative to controls but should not be treated as uniquely transformed relative to adjacent technical work.

Current evidence: T18 finds broad-AI growth of +27.2 pp for SWE, +27.0 pp for adjacent roles, and +2.6 pp for controls. AI-tool growth is +21.8 pp for SWE, +19.1 pp for adjacent roles, and +0.8 pp for controls. Requirement breadth rises +2.56 for SWE, +2.93 for adjacent roles, and +0.70 for controls in the LLM-labeled subset.

Required robustness checks:

  • Occupation-group definitions audited, especially QA/test, data engineering, ML, solutions, and support roles.
  • Exclude fragile SWE title_lookup_llm tier.
  • Company cap and aggregator exclusion.
  • Alternative adjacent groupings: data scientist/ML, data engineering, product/solutions/support, QA/test.
  • LLM-cleaned subset reporting for text-sensitive outcomes.
  • Within-2024 calibration for cross-source occupation differences.

Would falsify: Controls show expansion of similar magnitude after classification and source controls; adjacent movement disappears under stricter definitions; or the SWE-control difference is driven by classification drift rather than posting content.

docs.title: H3. Geographic diffusion rather than tech-hub concentration
docs.text:
Construct: Metro-level change in AI/tool prevalence and requirement breadth.

Expected direction: AI/tool prevalence increases in nearly all eligible metros, with tech hubs somewhat larger but not the only source of movement. Metro AI growth should travel more with requirement-breadth growth than with junior-share decline.

Current evidence: T17 reports broad-AI and AI-tool-specific increases in all 26 eligible metros. Mean broad-AI change is +17.6 pp; mean AI-tool-specific change is +16.1 pp. Broad-AI growth correlates with requirement-breadth growth (r = 0.44, p = 0.026) but not with J1 or J3 junior-share change.

Required robustness checks:

  • Metro threshold sensitivity, especially >=50 versus >=100 per period.
  • Exclude multi-location postings and report excluded denominators.
  • Company cap within metro-period.
  • Aggregator exclusion.
  • Arshkon-only historical baseline.
  • Metro fixed effects and domain/archetype-mix controls where coverage allows.
  • Explicit exclusion of remote-work claims until remote fields are audited.

Would falsify: A substantial subset of metros becomes flat or negative after company/source controls; the apparent diffusion is mostly a few high-volume employers replicated across metros; or metro changes are explained almost entirely by source composition.

docs.title: H4. Requirement and technology breadth expansion across seniority
docs.text:
Construct: Breadth of required technologies, AI-specific terms, organizational-scope language, and credential-stack categories across junior and senior definitions.

Expected direction: Requirement breadth and technology breadth rise across J1-J4 and S1/S4 definitions. The effect should be interpreted as broader listed stacks in longer/richer postings, not automatically as denser requirements per character.

Current evidence: T11 reports all-SWE requirement breadth rising from 7.23 to 9.39 (+2.16) in LLM-cleaned SWE rows. V1 independently verifies a similar result, 7.46 to 9.67 (+2.20). T11 reports junior breadth changes of J1 +1.74, J2 +1.84, J3 +1.75, J4 +2.08, and senior changes of S1 +2.40 and S4 +2.61. T14 finds technology-count increases across all junior and senior definitions.

Required robustness checks:

  • T30 seniority panel for every seniority claim.
  • LLM-cleaned denominator reporting, with coverage caveats.
  • Length controls and section-filtered text.
  • Aggregator exclusion, company cap, entry-specialist exclusion.
  • Arshkon-only and pooled-2024 baselines.
  • Source and SWE-tier sensitivity.
  • Manual validation for any new requirement families.

Would falsify: The breadth increase vanishes after length, section, source, and company controls; it appears only in raw fallback text; or the direction fails for both J1 and J3 or for both S1 and S4.

docs.title: H5. AI is integrating into existing stacks rather than replacing them
docs.text:
Construct: Co-occurrence of AI-tool/LLM terms with non-AI technologies and platform practices.

Expected direction: AI-tool/LLM postings mention more non-AI technologies and platform practices than non-AI postings, especially Python, API design, observability, cloud, and ML frameworks. Density may not rise after accounting for longer descriptions.

Current evidence: T14 reports that in 2026 AI-tool/LLM postings have mean non-AI tech count 7.63 versus 6.60 for non-AI postings. Among 2026 AI-tool/LLM rows, the largest traditional co-occurrence lifts include Python (+22.3 pp), API design (+18.2 pp), PyTorch (+12.3 pp), observability (+12.3 pp), and AWS (+10.4 pp). The 2026 network places Python, LLM, generative AI, RAG, PyTorch, Claude, TensorFlow, and MLOps in a coherent cluster.

Required robustness checks:

  • Length-normalized models and density outcomes.
  • Company-capped co-occurrence networks.
  • Broad AI-tool versus AI-domain separation.
  • Exclusion of ambiguous tokens.
  • Sensitivity to LLM-cleaned versus raw fallback text for binary tech screens.
  • Validation against asaniczka structured skills where possible.

Would falsify: AI-tool postings no longer have higher non-AI stack breadth after length and company controls; the AI/Python/platform cluster disappears under company spread thresholds; or AI terms appear mainly in company descriptions rather than role requirements.

docs.title: H6. Seniority labels and YOE floors are diverging labor-market signals
docs.text:
Construct: Agreement and divergence among employer labels, seniority_final, low-YOE requirements, and semantic neighborhood structure.

Expected direction: Explicit entry labels and low-YOE floors identify different populations. Low-YOE postings should be frequently unknown or mid-senior by label, and label-based and YOE-based junior trends may move in different directions in common-company panels.

Current evidence: T08 reports that in scraped 2026, 72.4% of yoe_extracted <= 2 rows are seniority_final = unknown, 21.5% are mid-senior, and only 5.5% are J1 entry. T16 reports common-company J1 entry share falling -0.44 pp while J3 low-YOE share rises +6.63 pp. T15 rejects generic junior-senior convergence but finds low-YOE J3/J4 rows are more senior-neighbor-heavy than label-based J1 rows.

Required robustness checks:

  • Explicit unknown-seniority profiling.
  • T30 J1-J4 and all/known denominators.
  • Title-level-code audit for L2/L3/L4/L5 under-labeling.
  • Company weighting and entry-specialist exclusion.
  • Arshkon-only versus pooled-2024 source restriction.
  • T20-style boundary model recovery, if possible.

Would falsify: J1 and J3/J4 have high overlap and move together under source and company controls; low-YOE rows are mostly entry-labeled after seniority audit; or semantic diagnostics show no difference between label-based entry and low-YOE rows.

docs.title: H7. Junior-role collapse is not the expected lead result
docs.text:
Construct: Junior share and volume under employer-label and low-YOE definitions.

Expected direction: No robust aggregate junior decline. If decline appears, it should be limited to specific label/source/company-panel estimands. The formal paper should test for boundary ambiguity and scope expansion, not assume junior disappearance.

Current evidence: T30 finds J1-J4 all rise from pooled 2024 to scraped 2026. T17 pooled geography mostly shows junior/low-YOE shares up across metros. T18 finds SWE J1-J4 all up. T16 finds arshkon-only common-company J1/J2 down but J3/J4 up, showing construct dependence.

Required robustness checks:

  • Report J1-J4 separately, not as one junior measure.
  • Source restriction: arshkon-only versus pooled 2024.
  • Row-weighted, company-weighted, and common-company estimands.
  • Entry-specialist and aggregator exclusion.
  • Volume and share outcomes separated.
  • Temporal stability once T19-style checks exist.

Would falsify: J1, J3, and J4 all decline across pooled, arshkon-only, common-company, company-weighted, and geography specifications, with effects above within-2024 calibration.

docs.title: H8. The market structure is domain/technology-first, seniority-second
docs.text:
Construct: Relative explanatory power of domain/archetype versus seniority for posting representations.

Expected direction: Technology/domain structure explains much more text and embedding variation than seniority. Formal models should include domain/archetype structure rather than treating junior/senior as the primary organizing axis.

Current evidence: T09's NMF archetypes align with tech_domain at NMI 0.205 and with seniority at only 0.0069. T15 finds archetype eta-squared of 0.111 in embeddings and 0.132 in TF-IDF/SVD, compared with seniority eta-squared of about 0.007. V1 verifies the domain-over-seniority pattern.

Required robustness checks:

  • LLM-cleaned coverage reporting and expansion if possible.
  • Alternative representations: embeddings, TF-IDF/SVD, NMF labels.
  • Company cap and source controls.
  • Domain-stratified breadth and AI models.
  • Do not use NMF labels as causal treatments.

Would falsify: Seniority explains representation variance at similar or greater magnitude than archetype/domain after source and company controls; or domain effects collapse when scraped LLM coverage is expanded.

docs.title: H9. Senior roles are broadening, not simply moving away from management
docs.text:
Construct: Senior-role requirement breadth, mentorship/coordination language, direct-report management language, and top-ladder labels.

Expected direction: Senior roles show greater requirement breadth and stack depth. Mentorship and coordination language rises or remains common, while direct-report management remains rare. A simple "senior management language declined" hypothesis is not supported.

Current evidence: T11 reports senior requirement breadth rising for S1 (+2.40) and S4 (+2.61). Strong-management indicators rise under S1/S3/S4, and T11 notes the top triggers are mentorship-heavy: mentor rises from 10.3% to 12.7%, while direct reports, performance reviews, hiring, and headcount remain rare. T30/T08 show broad senior labels and YOE-senior shares decline while director-only labels rise.

Required robustness checks:

  • Separate direct-report management, hiring/headcount, mentorship, coordination, architecture, review, and ownership.
  • Use S1 and S4 as primary senior definitions; treat S2 as sparse top-ladder evidence.
  • Avoid current normalized-title S3 as a standalone senior-title measure.
  • T21-style senior deep dive before paper-grade claims.
  • Manual validation of management and mentorship contexts.

Would falsify: Senior breadth does not rise under S1/S4 after company/source controls; mentorship/coordination language declines under validated patterns; or director-only growth is a source artifact with no top-ladder content signal.

docs.title: H10. A nontrivial share of AI/platform requirements may be aspirational or template-driven
docs.text:
Construct: Requirement force: screened must-have, preferred/nice-to-have, aspirational, template/default language, or employer branding.

Expected direction: Unknown from completed quantitative reports. The working hypothesis should be that some AI/tool/platform language reflects real screening and some reflects templates or anticipatory signaling. This is a validity hypothesis, not a demonstrated finding.

Current evidence: T22 failed and provides no direct evidence. T13, V1, and T16 reduce the probability that all movement is simple boilerplate or new-company mix, but they do not establish screened requirement force. T11 and V1 explicitly caution that regex features measure mentions, not required proficiency or screening intensity.

Required robustness checks:

  • Recover T22 or equivalent sentence-level force classification.
  • Distinguish required, preferred, responsibility, company background, and benefits/about sections.
  • Manual context validation for AI/tool/platform terms.
  • Compare hiring-manager rubrics and recruiter screens through interviews.
  • Employer-template and ATS-language provenance checks.

Would falsify: Nearly all validated AI/tool/platform mentions are in hard requirements and are screened in interviews; or, conversely, nearly all are company/background/template mentions and not role requirements, which would falsify a real hiring-bar interpretation.

docs.title: H11. Employer-requirement / worker-usage divergence remains a hypothesis, not a result
docs.text:
Construct: Gap between employer-side posting AI/tool requirements and external worker-side AI usage or exposure benchmarks.

Expected direction: Posting-side AI-tool and agentic-workflow requirements may outpace measured worker usage, especially for specific coding tools and agent workflows. This should be tested as a divergence index, not a causal treatment effect.

Current evidence: T23 failed. Completed reports establish a strong posting-side numerator for AI/tool requirements but do not compare it to worker usage. Gate 3 says RQ3 cannot be a lead claim yet.

Required robustness checks:

  • External usage benchmark choice documented and occupation-aligned.
  • Tool-specific, AI-domain, and generic AI measures kept separate.
  • Timing alignment between postings and usage data.
  • Sensitivity to occupational crosswalks and adjacent technical roles.
  • Report divergence as comparison of different objects, not direct unmet demand.

Would falsify: Worker usage is similar to or higher than employer AI-tool requirement prevalence on a comparable occupation/time basis; benchmarks are too coarse to support inference; or posting-side measures are mostly aspirational/template language.

docs.title: Formal Analysis Priorities Implied By The Hypotheses
docs.text:
1. Estimate company fixed-effect and common-company models for broad AI, AI-tool, tech breadth, and requirement breadth.
  2. Estimate cross-occupation DiD models for SWE, adjacent technical roles, and controls.
  3. Model metro diffusion as supporting evidence, not the main mechanism.
  4. Keep seniority hypotheses split across J1/J3 and S1/S4.
  5. Recover requirement-force evidence before making claims about actual hiring bars.
  6. Treat employer-worker divergence as a later-stage test unless T23-equivalent evidence is produced.

docs.title: Scope
docs.text:
This report turns the exploration findings into interview prompts and vignettes for recruiters, hiring managers, and engineers. It uses existing reports and memos only. No new data analysis, preprocessing, or LLM calls were run.

The goal is mechanism discovery and claim validation, not confirmation. The prompts should help distinguish real workflow change from screening inflation, templates, employer branding, anticipatory language, and measurement artifacts.

docs.title: Recruiters
docs.text:
Recruiters can explain how language enters postings, who edits requirements, how ATS/search incentives shape phrasing, which requirements are screened, and whether seniority labels match hiring-manager intent.

Useful respondent types:

  • Technical recruiters for SWE, data, ML, platform, DevOps, and adjacent technical roles.
  • Recruiting managers who maintain templates.
  • Agency/staffing recruiters who repost or translate employer requirements.

docs.title: Hiring Managers
docs.text:
Hiring managers can explain the actual bar, team adoption of AI tools, stack expectations, interview rubrics, and how low-YOE or senior roles are scoped.

Useful respondent types:

  • SWE managers and directors.
  • Platform, data, ML, DevOps, and infrastructure managers.
  • Managers at returning companies that changed JD language, if reachable.

docs.title: Engineers
docs.text:
Engineers can explain day-to-day tool use, whether posted requirements match work, how interviews test AI/tool skills, and whether junior/senior responsibilities shifted.

Useful respondent types:

  • Junior or low-YOE engineers.
  • Senior/staff engineers involved in interviewing.
  • Engineers in SWE-adjacent roles such as data engineering, ML, QA automation, solutions, platform, or DevOps.

docs.title: Cross-Cutting Interview Opener
docs.text:
Use this before any vignette:

Think about the most recent software, data, ML, platform, DevOps, or adjacent technical role you helped hire for, applied to, interviewed for, or worked in. Walk me through the role from the first description or recruiter screen to the actual day-to-day work.

Follow-up probes:

  • Who wrote or edited the job description?
  • Which requirements mattered enough to reject a candidate?
  • Which requirements were negotiable?
  • Which requirements were copied from a template?
  • What changed compared with similar roles a year or two earlier?
  • What would a strong candidate actually need to know on day one?

docs.title: Vignette
docs.text: A backend or platform posting asks for Python, cloud infrastructure, CI/CD, observability, and API design. It also mentions LLM APIs, RAG, Cursor or Copilot, prompt engineering, and AI agents. The posting does not clearly say which items are required and which are preferred.

docs.title: Recruiter prompts
docs.text:
• How would you screen a candidate for this role before a hiring-manager interview?
  • Which AI/tool items would be knockout criteria?
  • If a candidate has strong backend/platform experience but no RAG or agent experience, what happens?
  • Who usually adds AI tools to the posting: recruiter, hiring manager, HR template, employer brand, or another source?
  • Have you been asked to add AI language to roles where it was not part of the original manager request?

docs.title: Hiring-manager prompts
docs.text:
• Which AI/tool skills would you actually test in interviews?
  • What would count as evidence of practical ability: shipped feature, prototype, side project, code review, prompt skill, evaluation design, or tool familiarity?
  • Is AI-tool experience a substitute for traditional stack experience, an addition to it, or a signal of learning speed?
  • For your team, what changed first: the work, the interview rubric, or the posting language?

docs.title: Engineer prompts
docs.text:
• Have you seen AI/tool requirements in postings that did or did not match the actual job?
  • In interviews, were you asked to demonstrate AI-tool use, discuss it, or merely acknowledge familiarity?
  • Which tools do engineers actually use in your workflow, and which tools appear more in job ads than in work?
  • How do candidates learn whether an AI requirement is real?

docs.title: What this adjudicates
docs.text: Supports a real hiring-bar interpretation if respondents describe concrete screens, rubrics, or day-one work. Supports ghost/template interpretation if requirements are copied, weakly screened, or used mainly for signaling.

docs.title: Vignette
docs.text: A low-YOE software role asks for a programming language, cloud deployment, Kubernetes or Terraform, CI/CD pipelines, API design, observability, cross-functional collaboration, and familiarity with AI-assisted development. The role asks for two years of experience.

docs.title: Recruiter prompts
docs.text:
• When a low-YOE role lists this many technologies, how do you decide what to screen for?
  • Do you search for all listed technologies in resumes, or only a subset?
  • Are broader lists meant to widen the candidate pool, narrow it, or satisfy internal stakeholders?
  • Do candidates ask which requirements are truly required?

docs.title: Hiring-manager prompts
docs.text:
• Which parts of the stack should a two-year candidate already know?
  • Which parts can be learned after joining?
  • Has AI tooling changed what you expect a low-YOE engineer to own?
  • Are broader stack lists a response to smaller teams, faster onboarding expectations, more platform complexity, or something else?

docs.title: Engineer prompts
docs.text:
• Do recent postings expect more breadth than similar roles you saw earlier?
  • Which requirements felt reasonable versus inflated?
  • Did the interview test breadth, depth, or ability to navigate unknown tools?
  • For low-YOE engineers, what support exists when a posting asks for production ownership or broad platform knowledge?

docs.title: What this adjudicates
docs.text: Connects T11/T14 breadth increases to mechanisms: real scope expansion, team downsizing, platformization, screening inflation, or broad "familiarity" language.

docs.title: Vignette
docs.text: Two postings both ask for zero to two years of experience. One is labeled "entry level." The other is labeled "mid-senior" or has no seniority label, but its stated YOE is still low. Both include modern platform and AI/tool language.

docs.title: Recruiter prompts
docs.text:
• How do seniority labels get assigned on LinkedIn or in your ATS?
  • Would you treat a low-YOE posting without an entry label as an entry role?
  • Do hiring managers avoid the word "entry" even when they will consider low-YOE candidates?
  • Are labels ever chosen for search visibility, compensation banding, internal leveling, or approval workflows?

docs.title: Hiring-manager prompts
docs.text:
• What distinguishes an entry role from a low-YOE non-entry role on your team?
  • Would you hire a two-year candidate into a role labeled mid-level or senior if they had the right stack?
  • Are entry labels avoided because of mentoring load, headcount approvals, compensation bands, or candidate signaling?
  • Has AI changed the boundary between "can learn on the job" and "must already be productive"?

docs.title: Engineer prompts
docs.text:
• Have you applied to roles where the label and stated years of experience seemed inconsistent?
  • How did you interpret "entry," "associate," "software engineer," "mid-level," or "new grad" labels?
  • Did interview expectations match the label or the YOE number?
  • For low-YOE hires, what responsibilities were actually assigned?

docs.title: What this adjudicates
docs.text: Directly targets the T08/T16/T30 label-vs-YOE split. It can explain whether low-YOE non-entry postings are relabeling, internal leveling, platform artifact, or genuine boundary thickening.

docs.title: Vignette
docs.text: A data engineer, ML engineer, QA automation engineer, product/solutions engineer, or platform-adjacent role now lists GitHub workflows, CI/CD, APIs, cloud deployment, Python, LLM tools, model APIs, or AI-assisted development.

docs.title: Recruiter prompts
docs.text:
• Do you source these roles from SWE candidate pools, adjacent technical pools, or both?
  • Which requirements make an adjacent role look "software-producing" rather than purely analytical, support, or operations work?
  • Have hiring managers changed adjacent-role requirements faster than SWE requirements?
  • Do title labels lag behind actual technical expectations?

docs.title: Hiring-manager prompts
docs.text:
• Which adjacent roles on your team now require production software practices?
  • Are AI tools changing the boundary between data, ML, platform, and SWE work?
  • Do you expect adjacent-role candidates to know SDLC, version control, CI/CD, observability, or APIs?
  • Are these requirements screened like SWE requirements or treated as collaboration vocabulary?

docs.title: Engineer prompts
docs.text:
• Have adjacent technical roles become more software-like in practice?
  • Which engineering practices moved into your role?
  • Do postings overstate this boundary shift, understate it, or describe it accurately?
  • How does AI tooling affect collaboration between SWE and adjacent technical roles?

docs.title: What this adjudicates
docs.text: Connects T18's adjacent-parallel expansion to mechanisms. It tests whether the paper should frame the change as SWE-specific, SWE-amplified, or software-producing technical-work restructuring.

docs.title: Module 5: Ghost, Aspirational, And Template Language
Vignette
docs.text: A company updates many technical job descriptions to include AI-assisted development, LLMs, automation, workflows, and modern platform tooling. Recruiters and hiring managers disagree about whether these are real requirements, preferred skills, or forward-looking language.

docs.title: Recruiter prompts
docs.text:
• Can you identify which parts of a posting came from a template?
  • Are AI/tool phrases inserted centrally across many roles?
  • Are recruiters evaluated on keyword coverage, candidate volume, or hiring-manager satisfaction in ways that encourage broad lists?
  • Have you seen requirements left in a posting after a hiring manager said they were optional?
  • What language would you use to mark a requirement as "nice to have" versus truly required?

docs.title: Hiring-manager prompts
docs.text:
• When you approve a posting, do you remove requirements that are not screened?
  • Are any AI/tool requirements included because the team plans to adopt them later?
  • How often do candidates get hired without meeting the AI/platform requirements listed?
  • Which requirements would you defend to compensation, HR, or leadership as necessary for the role?

docs.title: Engineer prompts
docs.text:
• Which posted requirements were never mentioned in interviews or work?
  • Have you been asked to list tools in a resume because postings mention them, even if they are not central?
  • Do AI/tool requirements feel like actual practice, branding, or market fashion?
  • How do engineers internally talk about AI skills compared with how postings describe them?

docs.title: What this adjudicates
docs.text: This is the highest-priority qualitative validity module because T22 failed. It determines whether posting-language claims can be cautiously interpreted as hiring-bar claims.

docs.title: Module 6: Company Adoption And Within-Firm Change
Vignette
docs.text: A company that hired software engineers in 2024 now posts similar roles in 2026 with more AI/tool, platform, workflow, and stack-breadth language.

docs.title: Recruiter prompts
docs.text:
• Did the company change templates, hiring-manager instructions, or sourcing strategy around AI?
  • Was there a central policy about adding AI or automation language?
  • Do different teams at the same company use similar AI/tool language?
  • Are reposted or refreshed roles updated automatically?

docs.title: Hiring-manager prompts
docs.text:
• What triggered the wording change: tool adoption, leadership directive, team reorganization, candidate market, or template refresh?
  • Did expectations change for all roles or only for AI/product/platform teams?
  • Was the change tied to smaller teams, faster delivery expectations, or new evaluation responsibilities?
  • How would the same role have been described in 2024?

docs.title: Engineer prompts
docs.text:
• Did your team's actual workflow change before or after postings changed?
  • Was AI adoption voluntary, team-led, mandated, or uneven?
  • Did senior engineers become reviewers/orchestrators of AI-generated work?
  • Did juniors get more or less support as AI tools entered the workflow?

docs.title: What this adjudicates
docs.text: Targets T16's strongest mechanism result: within-company AI/tool expansion. It can distinguish real within-firm adoption from central template changes.

docs.title: Recruiter sequence
docs.text:
1. Recent role walk-through.
  2. JD authorship and approval chain.
  3. Required versus preferred screening list.
  4. AI/tool language provenance.
  5. Seniority label assignment.
  6. Candidate rejection examples.
  7. Template, ATS, reposting, and SEO incentives.
  8. Compare 2024-like role with current role.

docs.title: Hiring-manager sequence
docs.text:
1. Team workflow and current tool stack.
  2. Role scope and day-one expectations.
  3. Which requirements are screened and how.
  4. AI-tool adoption history.
  5. Low-YOE versus entry distinction.
  6. Senior responsibilities: mentorship, architecture, review, coordination, direct reports.
  7. Adjacent-role collaboration and boundary changes.
  8. Which posting language they would remove if forced to shorten the JD.

docs.title: Engineer sequence
docs.text:
1. Recent application/interview/work experience.
  2. Match between posting, interview, and work.
  3. AI/tool use in actual development.
  4. Breadth expectations and onboarding.
  5. Seniority-label interpretation.
  6. Mentorship, review, and AI-generated code practices.
  7. Adjacent-role collaboration.
  8. Examples of requirements that were real, optional, or never used.

docs.title: Coding Map For Interview Analysis
docs.text: Mechanism code Evidence that supports it Evidence that weakens it Screened AI/tool requirement Concrete interview task, rubric, rejection, day-one work Candidate hired without skill; requirement never discussed Aspirational requirement Future adoption plans; leadership wants signal before workflow exists Existing production use with clear screening Template inflation Central template, copy-paste, automatic refresh, recruiter default Hiring manager wrote role-specific requirement Broadened stack expectation Smaller teams, platform ownership, CI/CD/observability required Broad list is only keyword search or nice-to-have Low-YOE relabeling YOE low but entry label avoided for leveling/banding/recruiting reasons Label accurately maps to role scope and support Adjacent boundary thickening Adjacent roles now use SDLC/API/cloud/AI tools in production Adjacent roles only coordinate with SWE, no own production work Senior orchestration/review Seniors review AI-generated code, architecture, evaluation, mentoring Senior work unchanged or direct management dominates Employer-worker divergence Postings mention tools that workers rarely use Tool use common and posting language accurate

docs.title: Non-Leading Probes
docs.text:
• "Can you give me a concrete example?"
  • "Who made that decision?"
  • "What happened when a candidate lacked that requirement?"
  • "Was that checked before or after the hiring-manager interview?"
  • "Where did that phrase come from?"
  • "How would you rewrite the posting to match the actual job?"
  • "What would surprise a new hire after reading the posting?"
  • "What would be different for a two-year candidate versus a senior candidate?"

docs.title: Claims These Interviews Can And Cannot Support
docs.text:
Interviews can support mechanism claims about how postings are written, how requirements are screened, why labels diverge from YOE floors, and whether AI/tool language reflects real workflows or aspirational templates.

Interviews cannot by themselves estimate market-wide prevalence, employment effects, wage effects, or causal impacts of AI releases. They should be integrated as qualitative validation and mechanism evidence for the posting-content findings.

docs.title: Status
docs.text:
The main Wave 4 synthesis handoff is exploration/reports/SYNTHESIS.md. This T26 task report records the evidence boundary and points readers to the full synthesis.

No new data analysis, preprocessing, parquet inspection, or LLM calls were run. T26 is based on gate memos, T01-T18, T30, and V1, with Gate 3 as the primary strategic context.

docs.title: Core Narrative
docs.text: Between 2024 and 2026, SWE and software-adjacent technical postings do not show a robust junior-role collapse. The stronger evidence is that postings broadened around AI/tool/platform/workflow requirements across returning companies, metros, and adjacent technical occupations, while seniority labels and YOE floors diverged as measurement signals.

docs.title: Most Important Boundaries
docs.text:
• T19 is missing: no temporal-rate, scraped-window stability, posting-age, or backlog-vs-flow evidence.
  • T20/T21 are missing: no seniority-boundary classifier, associate-distance modeling, or senior-role deep dive.
  • T22/T23 are missing: no ghost/aspirational requirement-force analysis and no employer-worker usage divergence test.
  • V2 is missing: T16/T17/T18 have advisor synthesis but no independent verification report comparable to V1.
  • Text, archetype, semantic, and requirement-breadth claims remain limited by scraped LLM-cleaned coverage.

docs.title: Files
docs.text:
• Full synthesis: exploration/reports/SYNTHESIS.md
  • Hypotheses: exploration/reports/T24.md
  • Interview artifacts: exploration/reports/T25.md

docs.title: Scope
docs.text: Built a static, navigable evidence package under exploration/site/ using existing reports, memos, and figures only. No new data analysis, parquet inspection, preprocessing, or LLM calls were run.

docs.title: Evidence Boundary
docs.text: The package explicitly treats T19, T20/T21, T22/T23, and V2 as gaps. It does not claim junior collapse, ghost/aspirational prevalence, employer-worker usage divergence, temporal rates, seniority-boundary modeling, senior-role deep-dive results, or independent V2 verification.

docs.title: Headline
docs.text:
Junior definitions J1-J4 all move up from pooled 2024 to scraped 2026. Senior definitions are directionally split: S1, S3, and S4 move down, while director-only S2 moves up. The robust reading is "junior share rises across definitions, broad senior/YOE senior share falls, and director labels rise as a distinct top-ladder phenomenon."

The canonical panel exists at exploration/artifacts/shared/seniority_definition_panel.csv. I verified its schema with DuckDB: 44 rows, one row per definition x source-period for J1-J6 and S1-S5, with the required columns definition, side, period, source, n_of_all, n_of_known, share_of_all, share_of_known, mde_arshkon_vs_scraped, mde_pooled_vs_scraped, within_2024_effect, cross_period_effect, and direction.

Evidence base: existing T30 tables/figures in exploration/tables/T30/, exploration/figures/T30/, and the shared panel artifact. No panel regeneration was performed.

docs.title: Direction Consistency and MDE
docs.text:
Shares below use each definition's known denominator. Effects and MDEs are percentage-point changes.

Definition Known denominator Pooled 2024 share Scraped 2026 share Direction Cross-period effect Within-2024 effect MDE arshkon-vs-scraped MDE pooled-vs-scraped J1: seniority_final = entry known seniority 2.70% 6.70% up +3.99 -5.98 1.74 0.53 J2: final entry/associate known seniority 2.98% 6.97% up +3.99 -6.44 1.80 0.55 J3: yoe_extracted <= 2 YOE known 11.22% 17.19% up +5.98 -4.61 2.00 0.90 J4: yoe_extracted <= 3 YOE known 25.63% 33.74% up +8.12 -6.78 2.56 1.23 S1: final mid-senior/director known seniority 97.02% 93.03% down -3.99 +6.44 1.64 0.51 S2: final director known seniority 0.75% 1.55% up +0.80 +0.21 0.57 0.29 S3: normalized-title senior regex title known 1.95% 0.37% down -1.59 +0.26 0.61 0.33 S4: yoe_extracted >= 5 YOE known 65.28% 57.79% down -7.50 +6.30 2.66 1.33

Junior verdict: J1-J4 agree on direction. This is directionally robust, but the within-2024 source gap is not small. Signal-to-noise is below 2 for J1-J4, so downstream claims should describe this as consistent directional evidence, not as cleanly separated from historical-source instrument noise.

Senior verdict: S1-S4 do not all agree. S1, S3, and S4 decline; S2 rises. This is not a nuisance result. It suggests that director-only labels are behaving differently from broad senior labels and YOE-based seniority.

docs.title: Full Definition Notes
docs.text:
Additional panel rows:

Definition Pooled 2024 share Scraped 2026 share Direction Cross-period effect MDE pooled-vs-scraped J5: normalized-title junior regex 0.35% 1.70% up +1.34 0.15 J6: J1 union J5 1.67% 3.29% up +1.63 0.31 S5: yoe_extracted >= 8 23.94% 18.04% down -5.90 1.20

J5/J6 are useful diagnostics, not preferred headline definitions. S5 confirms that stricter YOE-based seniority also declines.

docs.title: Overlap Interpretation
docs.text:
Figures:

  • exploration/figures/T30/junior_overlap_heatmap.png
  • exploration/figures/T30/senior_overlap_heatmap.png

Junior overlap:

  • J1 is nested in J2 and J6 by construction.
  • J5 is high precision but narrow: 90.6% of J5 rows are also J1, while J5 captures only 42.4% of J1.
  • J3 is a subset of J4 by construction. The low J1/J3 row overlap is partly a missing-YOE artifact: the overlap matrix treats rows without extracted YOE as nonmatches.
  • J3/J4 are much broader than label-based junior definitions. They should be interpreted as "low stated YOE" rather than employer-posted junior labels.

Senior overlap:

  • S2 and S3 are both subsets of S1 in the observed panel.
  • S3 has only 596 rows overall under title_normalized, so it is not a useful high-recall senior title measure as currently computed.
  • S4 is related to S1 but not equivalent: 54.7% of S1 rows have yoe_extracted >= 5, and 65.4% of S4 rows are S1.
  • S5 is a strict subset of S4 and remains directionally down.

docs.title: Title-Keyword Spot Checks
docs.text:
J5-only-not-J1 has population n = 73. The existing 50-row sample is overwhelmingly real junior/new-grad title language: Graduate, New College Graduate, University Graduate, 2026 Start, and intern native labels dominate. I count 49 of 50 as semantically junior or new-grad; one row, Sr Systems Engineer (New Grad- Salt Lake City, UT), is contradictory and should be treated as ambiguous. Estimated J5-only precision from the existing sample: about 98%, with the caveat that many rows are concentrated in graduate hiring pipelines.

S3-only-not-S1 has no sampled rows because the population is zero: every normalized-title S3 row is already in S1. Precision for "S3 only" is therefore not estimable from the existing artifact.

Important artifact: title_keyword_raw_title_sensitivity.csv shows that raw-title senior matching is far larger than normalized-title S3. Raw-title S3 would be 56.7% of pooled 2024 SWE rows and 42.3% of scraped 2026 SWE rows, while normalized-title S3 is only 1.95% and 0.37%. The likely mechanism is that title_normalized strips seniority indicators. Do not use current S3 as a standalone senior-title prevalence measure.

docs.title: Aggregator Sensitivity
docs.text:
Excluding aggregators does not change directions:

Definition group Include aggregators Exclude aggregators J1-J4 all up all up S1 down down S2 up up S3 down down S4 down down

Magnitude shifts are modest for label-based junior definitions and larger for YOE seniority. For example, J1's known-denominator cross-period effect moves from +3.99 pp to +3.43 pp without aggregators; S4 moves from -7.50 pp to -8.90 pp. Aggregators are not driving the direction result, but they affect some effect sizes enough that Wave 2/3 should keep aggregator exclusion as a required sensitivity.

docs.title: Primary Recommendation
docs.text:
Primary junior definition: J1 (seniority_final = 'entry'). It is the cleanest employer-posted junior label, has strong YOE validity from T03, and agrees in direction with J2-J4. For subgroup/content analyses where J1 is thin, report J3 as the main label-independent robustness check rather than silently switching the estimand.

Primary senior definition: S1 (seniority_final IN ('mid-senior','director')). It is the best label-based broad-senior measure and is well powered. Always pair it with S4 (yoe_extracted >= 5) because S2 shows that director-only roles are moving differently, and current S3 is not a reliable title-keyword proxy.

docs.title: Surprises & Unexpected Patterns
docs.text:
• Junior direction is more consistent than expected: label-based and YOE-based definitions all rise.
  • The senior side is substantively split. Director-only rises while broad senior and YOE senior decline.
  • Normalized-title S3 is not measuring ordinary senior-title prevalence. The raw-title sensitivity implies a preprocessing/title-normalization artifact.
  • J2 adds little power over J1 in the current data, so combining entry and associate is not a major solution to thin junior cells.
  • Within-2024 calibration is large. Even when directions agree, historical-source variability is large enough to constrain Gate 1 claims.

docs.title: Findings First
docs.text:

Levels are within 3.2%; direction/effect verified. Use as a lead finding, but call it regex-derived requirement breadth in the LLM-cleaned subset. T11 J1-J4 junior breadth Verified direction; J1/J2 revised slightly upward in V1 V1: J1 +1.83, J2 +1.94, J3 +1.82, J4 +2.11. Reported: +1.74, +1.84, +1.75, +2.08. Keep unanimous direction. Treat exact J1/J2 magnitudes as definition-sensitive because independent regexes are slightly broader. T08/T14 broad AI prevalence Verified 3.751% -> 23.696% (+19.94 pp).
